This abstraction allows different types to be initialized from the same input type, providing a uniform interface for construction. In the context of cryptographic sponges, this is typically used to initialize states from a seed or initialization vector.

declarations.name: Code.UDRClose_iff_two_mul_proximity_lt_d_UDR

declarations.doc: Given an error/proximity parameter `e` within the unique decoding radius of a code `C` where `‖C‖₀ > 0`, this lemma proves the standard bound `2 * e < d` (i.e. condition of `Code.eq_of_lt_dist`).

declarations.name: Code.eq_of_le_uniqueDecodingRadius
declarations.doc: A stronger version of `distFromCode_eq_of_lt_half_dist`: If two codewords `v` and `w` are both within the `uniqueDecodingRadius` of `u` (i.e. `2 * Δ₀(u, v) < ‖C‖₀ and 2 * Δ₀(u, w) < ‖C‖₀`), then they must be equal.

declarations.name: Code.UDR_close_iff_exists_unique_close_codeword
declarations.doc: A word `u` is within the `uniqueDecodingRadius` of a code `C` if and only if there exists *exactly one* codeword `v` in `C` that is that close.

declarations.name: Code.UDR_close_iff_relURD_close

declarations.doc: A word `u` is close to a code `C` within the absolute unique decoding radius if and only if it is close within the relative unique decoding radius.

This is the canonical primitive for "coordinates where two words disagree", used throughout the coding-theory development. Several protocol-specific files (`Binius/BinaryBasefold/Prelude.lean`, `Stir/Quotienting.lean`, `Whir/BlockRelDistance.lean`, `DG25/MainResults.lean`) ship their own paper-shape `disagreementSet` with additional structure (interl

declarations.name: Code.dist
declarations.doc: The Hamming distance of a code `C` is the minimum Hamming distance between any two distinct elements of the code. We formalize this as the infimum `sInf` over all `d : ℕ` such that there exist `u v : n → R` in the code with `u ≠ v` and `hammingDist u v ≤ d`. If none exists, then we define the distance to be `0`.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.qEntropy
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Definition 2.2.** `q`-ary entropy function: `H_q(x) := x · log_q(q-1) - x · log_q(x) - (1-x) · log_q(1-x)`. For `q = 2` this reduces to the standard binary entropy function. Mathlib's convention `Real.log 0 = 0` makes the boundary cases `qEntropy q 0 = 0` and `qEntropy q 1 = log_q (q-1)` well-defined (treating `0 · log 0 = 0` and `log_q 1 = 0` automatically). **Boundary behaviour for `q ≤ 1`.** The paper assumes `q ≥ 2` (alphabet size of an error-correcting code). For `q ∈ {0, 1}`, `Real.logb q _` is identically `0` (since `Real.log q = 0` there), so `qEntropy 0 x = qEntropy 1 x = 0` r

declarations.name: LinearCode.fromRowGenMat
declarations.signature: def fromRowGenMat [Semiring F] (G : Matrix κ ι F) : LinearCode ι F :=
declarations.doc: Module code defined by left multiplication by its generator matrix. For a matrix `G : Matrix κ ι F` (over field `F`) and module `A` over `F`, this generates the `F`-submodule of `(ι → A)` spanned by the rows of `G` acting on `(κ → A)`. The matrix acts on vectors `v : κ → A` by : `(G • v)(i) = ∑ k, G k i • v k` where `G k i : F` is the scalar and `v k : A` is the module element.

declarations.name: LinearCode.IsMDS_iff_rate_distance
declarations.doc: **Bridge: `IsMDS` ↔ rate-distance form.** The `IsMDS` predicate (defined upstream in this file as the additive Nat form `Code.dist LC.carrier = length LC - dim LC + 1`) is equivalent to the rate-distance form `δ_min(LC) / n = 1 - ρ + 1/n` where `ρ = k/n` is the rate. The latter is the form ABF26 uses throughout §2-§3 (Lemma 2.6, Corollary 3.3). Requires `[Nonempty ι]` so `(Fintype.card ι : ℝ) ≠ 0`.

declarations.name: poly_eq_zero_of_dist_lt
declarations.signature: lemma poly_eq_zero_of_dist_lt {n k : ℕ} {F : Type*} [DecidableEq F] [CommRing F] [IsDomain F]

declarations.name: CoreResults.Matrix.IsMDS
declarations.signature: def Matrix.IsMDS [CommRing F] (G : Matrix (Fin k) (Fin n) F) : Prop :=
declarations.doc: A `k × n` matrix is MDS (Maximum Distance Separable) if every square `k × k` submatrix obtained by selecting `k` columns has nonzero determinant. Equivalently, every set of `k` columns is linearly independent.

declarations.name: CoreResults.minWt_ge_of_MDS
declarations.signature: lemma minWt_ge_of_MDS [Field F] [DecidableEq F] {G : Matrix (Fin k) (Fin n) F}
declarations.doc: Every nonzero codeword in a code generated by a `k × n` MDS matrix has Hamming weight greater or equal to `n - k + 1`.

declarations.name: CoreResults.vecMul_injective_of_rank_eq
declarations.signature: lemma vecMul_injective_of_rank_eq [Field F] {G : Matrix (Fin k) (Fin n) F} (hrank : G.rank = k) :
declarations.doc: If `G` has full row rank, then every nonzero vector maps to a nonzero codeword.

declarations.name: CoreResults.IsMDS_of_matrix_IsMDS
declarations.signature: lemma IsMDS_of_matrix_IsMDS [Field F] [DecidableEq F] {G : Matrix (Fin k) (Fin n) F}
declarations.doc: If a generator matrix is MDS with at least one row, then the code it generates is MDS.

declarations.name: CoreResults.matrix_IsMDS_of_IsMDS
declarations.signature: lemma matrix_IsMDS_of_IsMDS [Field F] [DecidableEq F] {G : Matrix (Fin k) (Fin n) F}
declarations.doc: If a linear code generated by a full-rank matrix is MDS, then the matrix is MDS.

declarations.name: CoreResults.colRank_genMatrix_eq_dim_of_MDS
declarations.doc: A linear code `LC` of length `ι` and dimension `dim` over a field `F` is MDS if any `dim` columns of the generator matrix whose rows are an `F`-basis of `LC` are linearly independent.[GRS25] Equivalently, a linear code is MDS if and only if its generator matrix is MDS. Note: the hypothesis `0 < dim LC` is necessary because for a trivial code `(dim = 0)`, `Matrix.IsMDS` is vacuously true while `IsMDS` requires positive distance.

declarations.name: Code.relHammingDist
declarations.signature: def relHammingDist (u v : ι → F) : ℚ≥0 :=

declarations.name: Code.restrictedRelHammingDist
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Definition 2.3.** Restricted (fractional) Hamming distance: `Δ_T(f, g) = Pr_{i ← T}[f i ≠ g i]`, equivalently the fraction of positions in `T` on which `f` and `g` differ. By `NNReal`'s `0 / 0 = 0` convention this returns `0` when `T = ∅`, matching the intuition that "the empty distribution agrees vacuously". Unlike `relHammingDist` (which divides by `Fintype.card ι` and requires `[Nonempty ι]`), this version explicitly takes a Finset `T : Finset ι` and divides by `T.card`.

declarations.name: Code.restrictedRelHammingDist_univ
declarations.doc: **Bridge between `restrictedRelHammingDist` and `relHammingDist`.** When `T = Finset.univ`, the restricted version coincides with the standard `relHammingDist` (cast to `ℝ≥0`). Lets downstream theorems convert freely between the ABF26 `Δ_T(f, g)` form and the existing `δᵣ(u, v)` notation.

declarations.name: Code.relDistFromCode

declarations.doc: The relative Hamming distance from a vector to a code, defined as the infimum of all relative distances from `u` to codewords in `C`. The type is `ENNReal` (ℝ≥0∞) to correctly handle the case `C = ∅`. For case of Nonempty C, we can use `relDistFromCode (δᵣ')` for smaller value range in `ℚ≥0`, which is equal to this definition.

declarations.doc: The relative distance to a code is at most the relative distance to any specific codeword.

declarations.doc: A word `u` is close to a code `C` within an absolute error bound `e` if and only if it is close within the equivalent relative error bound `e / n`.

declarations.doc: A word `u` is relatively close to a code `C` within an relative error bound `δ` if and only if it is relatively close within the equivalent absolute error bound `⌊δ * n⌋`.

declarations.name: Code.relDist_floor_bound_iff_complement_bound
declarations.doc: The equivalence between the two lowerbound of `upperBound` in Nat and NNReal context. In which, `upperBound` is viewed as the size of an **agreement set** `S` (e.g. between two words, or between a word to a code, ...). Specifically, `n - ⌊δ * n⌋ ≤ (upperBound : ℕ) ↔ (1 - δ) * n ≤ (upperBound : ℝ≥0)`. This lemma is useful for jumping back-and-forth between absolute distance and relative distance realms, especially when we work with an agreement set. For example, it can be used after `simp`ing with `closeToWord_iff_exists_agreementCols` and `relCloseToWord_iff_exists_agreementCols`.

declarations.name: Code.minRelHammingDistCode
declarations.doc: The minimum relative Hamming distance of a code. Uses `Set.Finite.toFinset` directly (via `finite_possibleRelHammingDists`) rather than a local `haveI : Fintype …`, so the underlying Finset doesn't depend on a typeclass-synthesised Fintype instance. This avoids a `Fintype.ofFinite` diamond with downstream proofs that need to manipulate `Finset.min'` of this set.

declarations.name: Code.minDist_div_card_eq_minRelHammingDistCode
declarations.doc: **Bridge: `Code.minDist C / n = δᵣ C` (cast to `ℚ`).** The raw `Code.minDist C / Fintype.card ι` form (used in ABF26 §3 theorems T3.2, C3.3, etc. as `δ_min`) coincides with the existing `minRelHammingDistCode C = δᵣ C` defined via `Finset.min'` over the rational-valued image. Both equal `min { hammingDist u v / n | u, v ∈ C, u ≠ v }`. **Boundary case.** When `C` is a subsingleton (no distinct codewords), both sides are `0` by the empty-`sInf` / else-branch conventions.

declarations.doc: Berlekamp-Welch decoder for Reed-Solomon codes. Given received codeword evaluations with potential errors, attempts to recover the original polynomial message or returns `none` if decoding fails. ### Parameters: - `e : ℕ` - Maximum number of correctable errors - `k : ℕ` - Degree bound of message polynomial (`<k`) - `[NeZero n]` - Instance that codeword length `n` is non-zero - `ωs : Fin n → F` - Evaluation points used during encoding - `f : Fin n → F` - Received (possibly corrupted) codeword evaluations ### Returns: - `some p` if successful (where `p : Polynomial F` is the recovered polynomial

declarations.doc: If the Berlekamp-Welch decoder succeeds, the decoded polynomial is within the error bound. ### Parameters: - `[NeZero n]` - Typeclass ensuring non-zero codeword length - `ωs : Fin n → F` - Evaluation points used in encoding - `f : Fin n → F` - Received (possibly corrupted) codeword - `h` - Hypothesis that decoder succeeded (returns `some p`)

declarations.name: BerlekampWelch.decoder_eq_some
declarations.signature: theorem decoder_eq_some {e k : ℕ} [NeZero n] {ωs f : Fin n → F} {p : Polynomial F}
declarations.doc: **Correctness theorem for Berlekamp-Welch decoder**: If a codeword is close to a polynomial `p` of degree `< k` then the decoder succeeds and returns `some p`. ### Parameters: - `e k : ℕ` - Error capacity and degree bound - `[NeZero n]` - Non-zero codeword length - `ωs : Fin n → F` - Distinct evaluation points (injective mapping) - `f : Fin n → F` - Received word - `p : Polynomial F` - Original polynomial message

declarations.name: BerlekampWelch.not_exists_of_decoder_eq_none
declarations.signature: theorem not_exists_of_decoder_eq_none {e k : ℕ} [NeZero n] {ωs f : Fin n → F}
declarations.doc: **Decoding failure characterization**: When the decoder returns `none`, no valid polynomial exists within the error bound. ### Theorem Statement: Given the decoder fails (`decoder e k ωs f = none`), there cannot exist any polynomial `p` of degree < `k` that is within `e` Hamming errors of the received word `f`. ### Parameters: - `e k : ℕ` - Error capacity and degree bound - `[NeZero n]` - Non-zero codeword length - `ωs : Fin n → F` - Evaluation points - `f : Fin n → F` - Received word

Solves the matrix equation A·x = b for x, where: - A is an n × m coefficient matrix - b is a length-n RHS vector Returning either: - `some x` if a solution exists - `none` if the system is inconsistent ### Parameters: - `A : Matrix (Fin n) (Fin m) F` - Coefficient matrix with dimensions n × m - `b : Fin n → F` - Right-hand side vector of length n ### Returns: - `some x` where `x : Fin m → F` is a solution vector if one exists - `none` if the system has no solution ### Behavior: 1. For consistent systems (solutions exist): - Retur

declarations.name: CodingTheory.linear_listSize_to_epsMCA_gcxk25
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 5.1 [GCXK25 Theorem 3].** List decoding implies MCA. Let `C ⊆ F^n` be a linear code and let `δ, η ∈ (0, 1)`. If `|Λ(C, δ)| ≤ L`, then `ε_mca(C, 1 - √(1 - δ + η)) ≤ (L²·δ·n + 1/η) / |F|` The conclusion's proximity radius `1 - √(1 - δ + η)` is the "Johnson lift" of `δ` (plus the `η` slack). For Reed-Solomon codes this implies MCA up to the "2 Johnson" regime via Corollary 3.3; for random RS codes (which list-decode to capacity by Theorem 3.6) it implies MCA for random RS up to the Johnson bound. **Paper divergence — added hypothesis `η ≤ δ`.** Paper T5.1 only requires `δ, η ∈ (0,

declarations.name: CodingTheory.rs_epsCA_small_implies_lambda_lt_F_bchks25
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 5.2 [BCHKS25 Theorem 1.9].** Small CA error implies small list size. Let `C := RS[F, L, k]` be a Reed-Solomon code with rate `ρ` and let `δ ∈ (0, 1-ρ)`. If `ε_ca(C, δ_fld = δ + 2/n, δ_int = 1 - ρ - 1/n) < 1/(2n)` then `|Λ(C, δ)| < |F|` . Reading: CA at `δ + 2/n` with proximity loss to `1 - ρ - 1/n` having very small error forces the list size at `δ` to be strictly below the field size. Admitted as an external result.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.rs_epsCA_implies_lambda_extended_cs25
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 5.3 [CS25 Theorem 2].** CA error converts to list size for related RS. Let `C := RS[F, L, k]` and `C⁺ := RS[F, L, k+1]` be Reed-Solomon codes with `|L| = n`. For `δ ∈ (0, δ_min(C))` and `η ∈ [0, 1)`, if `ε_ca(C, δ) ≤ η · (1/k - n/(k·|F|))` then `|Λ(C⁺, δ)| ≤ ⌈|F|/(1-η) · ε_ca(C, δ)⌉` Pivots CA on `C` to a list-size bound on the extended code `C⁺`. Admitted as an external result.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.rs_epsCA_separation_bgks20
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 5.4 [BGKS20 Lemma 3.3].** List decoding does **not** tightly imply CA. For all fields `F` of characteristic 2, the Reed-Solomon code `C := RS[F, F, |F|/8]` of rate `ρ = 1/8` (using `F` itself as the evaluation domain — a "full-domain" RS) satisfies `ε_ca(C, 1 - ρ^{1/3}) ≥ 1 - 1/|F|` . In particular `1 - ρ^{1/3} = 1 - (1/8)^{1/3} = 0.5`; the Johnson bound for the same code sits at `1 - √ρ - η ≈ 0.55`, where the list size is `≈ 40` (constant in `|F|`). This witnesses a code that is list-decodable at the Johnson radius yet has CA error ≈ 1 at a smaller radius — separating list dec

declarations.name: DivergenceOfSets.divergence
declarations.signature: def divergence (U V : Set (ι → F)) [Nonempty V] [Fintype V] : ℚ≥0 :=
declarations.doc: Definition of divergence of two sets from Section 1.2 in [BCIKS20]. For two sets `U` and `V`, the divergence of `U` from `V` is the maximum of the possible relative Hamming distances between elements of `U` and the set `V`.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.SupportsErasureCorrection
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Definition 6.4** (erasure-correction predicate). A code `C ⊆ (ι → F)` supports **erasure correction with correction time `ecor`** if there exists a deterministic algorithm `E_C` that, on any input `f : ι → Option F`: (i)  if `f` has strictly fewer than `δ_min(C) · |ι|` erasures and there exists a (necessarily unique) codeword `u ∈ C` agreeing with `f` off the erasures, then `E_C(f) = some u`; (ii) otherwise `E_C(f) = none`. Clause (ii) — easy to miss in a quick port from the paper — is what makes the predicate non-vacuous: without it, `E := fun _ ↦ some <arbitrary>` satisfies the recov

declarations.name: CodingTheory.ExtensionFieldPresentation
declarations.signature: structure ExtensionFieldPresentation (B F : Type) [Field B] [Field F] [Algebra B F] where
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Definition 2.19.** An *extension field presentation* is the data of a finite `B`-basis of `F`, in the presence of a `B`-algebra structure on `F`: - `B` and `F` are fields, - `[Algebra B F]` provides the embedding `ψ := algebraMap B F : B →+* F` and the `B`-module structure on `F`, - `e : ℕ` is the dimension of `F` as a `B`-vector space, - `basis : Basis (Fin e) B F` witnesses the `B`-linear isomorphism `F ≃ₗ[B] (Fin e → B)` (via `basis.equivFun`). This is a thin structure on top of Mathlib's existing `Algebra` / `Basis` machinery. The paper's named maps `ψ` and `φ` are derived (not dup

declarations.name: CodingTheory.ExtensionFieldPresentation._anon_def_84
declarations.signature: def def ψ (_P : ExtensionFieldPresentation B F) : B →+* F := algebraMap B F
declarations.doc: The base-field embedding `ψ : B ↪ F`, derived from `[Algebra B F]`.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.ExtensionFieldPresentation._anon_lemma_88
declarations.signature: lemma lemma ψ_injective (P : ExtensionFieldPresentation B F) : Function.Injective P.ψ :=
declarations.doc: Injectivity of `ψ` — automatic since the algebra map between fields is always injective.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.ExtensionFieldPresentation._anon_def_93
declarations.signature: def noncomputable def φ (P : ExtensionFieldPresentation B F) : F ≃ₗ[B] (Fin P.e → B) :=
declarations.doc: The `B`-linear coordinate isomorphism `φ : F ≃ₗ[B] (Fin e → B)`, derived from the basis.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.ExtensionFieldPresentation.coord
declarations.signature: def coord (P : ExtensionFieldPresentation B F) (j : Fin P.e) : F →ₗ[B] B :=
declarations.doc: The `j`-th coordinate `φᵢ : F →ₗ[B] B` of an extension-field presentation, as a `B`-linear map.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.ExtensionFieldPresentation.IsSystematic
declarations.signature: def IsSystematic (P : ExtensionFieldPresentation B F) : Prop :=
declarations.doc: A presentation is *systematic* if `φ(ψ(x)) = (x, 0, …, 0)` for every `x : B`. This makes the base-field copy of `B` inside `F` align with the first coordinate.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.ExtensionFieldPresentation.coord_add
declarations.signature: lemma coord_add (P : ExtensionFieldPresentation B F) (j : Fin P.e) (x y : F) :
declarations.doc: Each coordinate `P.coord j` is additive — direct consequence of being a `LinearMap`.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.ExtensionFieldPresentation.coord_psi_smul
declarations.doc: Each coordinate `P.coord j` respects the `B`-action — direct consequence of being a `B`-linear map. The `algebraMap`-based smul (`ψ b * x = b • x`) folds into ordinary `B`-scalar multiplication via `Algebra.smul_def`.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.extensionCode
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Definition 2.20.** The *extension code* `C_F : F^k → F^n` associated to a linear code `C_B : B^k → B^n` via an extension-field presentation. Defined on a vector `v : ι → F` by `v ∈ C_F ↔ ∀ j : Fin e, (fun i ↦ P.coord j (v i)) ∈ C_B` i.e. each of the `e` coordinate-projections of `v` lies in `C_B`. **Closure properties.** With `[Algebra B F]` + `Basis (Fin e) B F` from the refactored `ExtensionFieldPresentation`, `extensionCode P C_B` is closed under **both** addition (when `C_B` is) and `F`-scalar multiplication (when `C_B` is `B`-linear). See `extensionCode_add_mem` and `extensionCode

declarations.name: CodingTheory.extensionCode_iff_coord_in_base
declarations.doc: **Bridge to paper's encoder-image view.** A vector `v : ι → F` is in `extensionCode P C_B` iff each of its `e` coordinate-projections lies in `C_B`.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.extensionCode_add_mem
declarations.doc: **`extensionCode` is closed under addition** when `C_B` is. Uses `LinearMap.map_add` of the coordinate maps.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.extensionCode_psi_smul_mem
declarations.doc: **`extensionCode` is closed under the `ψ`-induced `B`-scalar action** when `C_B` is `B`-scalar closed. Uses `LinearMap.map_smul` of the coordinate maps.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.extensionCode_smul_mem
declarations.doc: **F-scalar closure of `extensionCode`** — the paper's D2.20 F-linearity claim, closed via the basis-expansion argument. **Proof outline.** For `α : F` and `v ∈ extensionCode P C_B`: 1. Write `α` in the basis: `α = ∑ k, (P.basis.repr α k) • (P.basis k)` via `Basis.sum_repr`. The coefficients `c_k := P.basis.repr α k` live in `B`. 2. Distribute: `α * v i = ∑ k, c_k • (P.basis k * v i)`. 3. Coordinate-by-coordinate, `P.coord j (α * v i) = ∑ k, c_k * P.coord j (P.basis k * v i)`. 4. Each `(fun i ↦ P.coord j (P.basis k * v i))` is itself a `B`-linear combination of `(fun i ↦ P.coord m (v i))`s (sin

declarations.name: CodingTheory.extensionCodeSubmodule
declarations.doc: **Submodule-packaging of `extensionCode`** when `C_B` is a `B`-submodule. Bundles the three closure laws (`add_mem`, `zero_mem`, `smul_mem`) into a single `Submodule F (ι → F)`, mirroring the `ReedSolomon.code` pattern (which returns a `Submodule F (ι → F)` directly). Downstream code that wants to consume an extension code as a linear code should use this form rather than the raw `Set`-based `extensionCode`. Built directly from the existing closure lemmas — no parallel implementation. The `Set`-form `extensionCode` is the carrier.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.coe_extensionCodeSubmodule
declarations.doc: The carrier of `extensionCodeSubmodule P C_B` coincides with the `Set`-form `extensionCode P (C_B : Set _)` — by construction.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.lambda_extensionCode_eq_lambda_interleaved
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Lemma 2.21 [BCFW25 Lemma D.3].** List size of an extension code equals the list size of the corresponding interleaved base code. Let `C_B : B^k → B^n` be a linear code and `P` be an extension-field presentation. For every `δ ∈ (0, 1)`: `|Λ(C_F, δ)| = |Λ(C_B^≡e, δ)|` where `C_F` is the extension code (D2.20) and `C_B^≡e` is the `e`-fold interleaved base code (D2.9). Admitted as an external result.

declarations.name: proximity_gap_degree_bound
declarations.signature: def proximity_gap_degree_bound (k n m : ℕ) : ℕ :=
declarations.doc: The degree bound (i.e. `D_X(m) = (m + 1/2) * √ρ * n`) for instantiation of Guruswami-Sudan in Lemma 5.3 of [BCIKS20].

declarations.name: proximity_gap_johnson
declarations.signature: def proximity_gap_johnson (k n m : ℕ) : ℝ :=
declarations.doc: The relative decoding radius (i.e. `δ₀(ρ, m) = 1 - √ρ - √ρ/2m` in Lemma 5.3 of [BCIKS20]). It follows from the Johnson bound.

declarations.name: gs_degree_bound
declarations.signature: def gs_degree_bound (k n m : ℕ) : ℕ :=
declarations.doc: Degree bound with ρ = k/n (matching RS code rate). The original `proximity_gap_degree_bound` uses ρ = (k+1)/n which is conservative.

declarations.name: gs_johnson
declarations.signature: def gs_johnson (k n m : ℕ) : ℝ :=
declarations.doc: Johnson radius with ρ = k/n. Approaches `1 - √(k/n)` as `m → ∞`.

declarations.name: gs_degree_bound_div_lt
declarations.signature: lemma gs_degree_bound_div_lt {k n F : ℕ} (hk : 2 ≤ k) (hn : n ≤ F) (hF : 5 ≤ F)
declarations.doc: The GS degree bound with m=1 divided by (k-1) is less than F when |F| ≥ 5 and the RS code is non-degenerate (k+1 ≤ n ≤ F).

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.monomial
declarations.doc: The monomial X^i Y^j as a bivariate polynomial.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.weigthBoundIndices
declarations.signature: def weigthBoundIndices (k D : ℕ) : Finset (ℕ × ℕ) :=
declarations.doc: Given a nonnegative integer `D`, it is the set of indices `(i,j)` such that `i + (k - 1) * j ≤ D`.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.numVars
declarations.signature: def numVars (k D : ℕ) : ℕ := (weigthBoundIndices k D).card
declarations.doc: The number of variables in the Guruswami-Sudan linear system.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.card_weigthBoundIndices_eq_sum
declarations.doc: The number of variables is the sum over j of the number of valid i's.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.numVars_eq_of_gt_one
declarations.doc: Closed form for the number of variables when k > 1.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.numVars_eq_sq
declarations.signature: lemma numVars_eq_sq {k D : ℕ} (hk : k ≤ 1) : numVars k D = (D + 1) ^ 2 := by
declarations.doc: The number of variables is (D+1)^2 when k ≤ 1.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.numVars_lower_bound_tight
declarations.doc: A tighter lower bound for the number of variables when k > 1 : 2(k-1) * numVars ≥ D(D+2).

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.constraintIndices
declarations.doc: Given a positive integer `m`, the set of derivative indices `(s,t)` such that `s + t < m`.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.numConstraints
declarations.signature: def numConstraints (n m : ℕ) : ℕ := n * (constraintIndices m).card
declarations.doc: The number of constraints in the Guruswami-Sudan linear system.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.card_constraintIndices
declarations.signature: lemma card_constraintIndices (m : ℕ) : (constraintIndices m).card = m * (m + 1) / 2 := by
declarations.doc: The indices of constraints are `m * (m + 1) / 2`.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.proximity_gap_degree_bound_sq_gt
declarations.doc: Lower bound for the square of (D+1). Specifically, (D+1)^2 > (m+1/2)^2 * (k+1) * n.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.coeffsToPoly
declarations.signature: def coeffsToPoly (k D : ℕ) : ((weigthBoundIndices k D) → F) →ₗ[F] F[X][Y] :=
declarations.doc: The linear map from the space of coefficients to polynomials.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.evalConstraint
declarations.signature: def evalConstraint (x y : F) (s t : ℕ) : F[X][Y] →ₗ[F] F where
declarations.doc: The linear map evaluating the (s,t)-th derivative coefficient at (x,y).

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.constraintMap
declarations.signature: def constraintMap (k n m : ℕ) (ωs : Fin n ↪ F) (f : Fin n → F) (D : ℕ) :
declarations.doc: The linear map representing the system of linear equations.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.exists_nonzero_solution
declarations.signature: lemma exists_nonzero_solution (k n m : ℕ) (ωs : Fin n ↪ F) (f : Fin n → F) :
declarations.doc: There exists a non-zero polynomial satisfying the conditions.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.exists_nonzero_solution_gen
declarations.signature: lemma exists_nonzero_solution_gen (k n m : ℕ) (ωs : Fin n ↪ F) (f : Fin n → F) (D : ℕ)
declarations.doc: Generalized existence: non-zero kernel element for arbitrary degree bound D, given numVars k D > numConstraints n m.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.polySol
declarations.signature: def polySol (k n m : ℕ) (ωs : Fin n ↪ F) (f : Fin n → F) : F[X][Y] :=
declarations.doc: The polynomial solution constructed from the non-zero kernel element.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.gs_polySol
declarations.signature: def gs_polySol (k n m : ℕ) (ωs : Fin n ↪ F) (f : Fin n → F)
declarations.doc: Polynomial solution with rate-corrected degree bound (ρ = k/n).

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.coeff_linearCombination_monomial
declarations.signature: lemma coeff_linearCombination_monomial (c : ℕ × ℕ →₀ F) (i j : ℕ) :
declarations.doc: The coefficient of X^i Y^j in a linear combination of monomials is the coefficient of the combination.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.linearIndependent_monomials
declarations.doc: The monomials are linearly independent.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.polySol_ne_zero
declarations.doc: The solved polynomial is non-zero.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.natWeightedDegree_monomial
declarations.signature: lemma natWeightedDegree_monomial (i j u v : ℕ) :
declarations.doc: The weighted degree of a monomial X^i Y^j is u*i + v*j.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.natWeightedDegree_monomial_eq
declarations.signature: lemma natWeightedDegree_monomial_eq (i j u v : ℕ) :
declarations.doc: The weighted degree of a monomial X^i Y^j is u*i + v*j.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.natWeightedDegree_add_le
declarations.signature: lemma natWeightedDegree_add_le (p q : F[X][Y]) (u v : ℕ) :
declarations.doc: The weighted degree of a sum is at most the maximum of the weighted degrees.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.natWeightedDegree_sum_le
declarations.signature: lemma natWeightedDegree_sum_le {ι : Type*} (s : Finset ι) (f : ι → F[X][Y]) (u v : ℕ) :
declarations.doc: The weighted degree of a sum is bounded by the supremum of the weighted degrees.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.natWeightedDegree_smul_le
declarations.signature: lemma natWeightedDegree_smul_le {F : Type} [Semiring F] (a : F) (p : F[X][Y]) (u v : ℕ) :
declarations.doc: The weighted degree of a scalar multiple is at most the weighted degree of the polynomial.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.natWeightedDegree_coeffsToPoly_le
declarations.signature: lemma natWeightedDegree_coeffsToPoly_le (k D : ℕ) (c : (weigthBoundIndices k D) → F) :
declarations.doc: The weighted degree of the polynomial constructed from coefficients is bounded by D.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.polySol_weightedDegree_le
declarations.doc: The solved polynomial has weighted degree at most the proximity gap degree bound.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.eval_eq_zero_of_constraint_zero
declarations.signature: lemma eval_eq_zero_of_constraint_zero {f : F[X][Y]} {x y : F} {m : ℕ} (hm : 1 ≤ m)
declarations.doc: If constraints vanish up to order m ≥ 1, the polynomial vanishes at the point.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.polySol_roots
declarations.signature: lemma polySol_roots {ωs : Fin n ↪ F} {f : Fin n → F} (hm : 1 ≤ m) (i : Fin n) :
declarations.doc: The solved polynomial vanishes at the interpolation points if m ≠ 0.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.list_min_le_of_mem
declarations.signature: lemma list_min_le_of_mem {l : List ℕ} {a m : ℕ} (h_min : l.min? = some m) (h_mem : a ∈ l) :
declarations.doc: If `m` is the minimum of a list `l`, then `m ≤ a` for any `a ∈ l`.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.rootMultiplicity_le_of_coeff_ne_zero
declarations.signature: lemma rootMultiplicity_le_of_coeff_ne_zero [DecidableEq F] {Q : F[X][Y]} {x y : F} {s t : ℕ}
declarations.doc: If the `(s, t)`-coefficient of `shift Q x y` is non-zero, then the root multiplicity of `Q` at `(x, y)` is at most `s + t`.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.shift_eq_zero_iff
declarations.signature: lemma shift_eq_zero_iff {F : Type} [Field F] (f : F[X][Y]) (x y : F) : shift f x y = 0 ↔ f = 0 := by
declarations.doc: Shifting a polynomial by (x, y) results in the zero polynomial if and only if the original polynomial was zero.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.rootMultiplicity_ge_of_shift_zero
declarations.signature: lemma rootMultiplicity_ge_of_shift_zero [DecidableEq F] {f : F[X][Y]} {x y : F}
declarations.doc: If the shifted polynomial has no non-zero coefficients of total degree less than m, then the root multiplicity is at least m.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.degree_eval_le_weightedDegree
declarations.signature: lemma degree_eval_le_weightedDegree (Q : F[X][Y]) (P : F[X]) (k : ℕ) (hP : P.natDegree ≤ k - 1) :
declarations.doc: The degree of Q(X, P(X)) is bounded by the (1, k-1)-weighted degree of Q, provided deg(P) ≤ k - 1.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.dvd_eval_of_rootMultiplicity_zero
declarations.doc: If Q has high multiplicity at (0,0) (meaning all coefficients c_{i,j} with i+j < m are zero) and P(0)=0, then Q(X, P(X)) is divisible by X^m.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.eval_shifted_eq_shifted_eval
declarations.signature: lemma eval_shifted_eq_shifted_eval (Q : F[X][Y]) (P : F[X]) (x y : F) :
declarations.doc: Evaluating the shifted bivariate polynomial at the shifted univariate polynomial is equivalent to shifting the result of the evaluation.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.HasOrderAt
declarations.signature: def HasOrderAt (Q : F[X][Y]) (x y : F) (m : ℕ) : Prop :=
declarations.doc: If Q vanishes to order m at (x, P(x)), then Q(X, P(X)) vanishes to order m at x.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.orderAt_eval_ge
declarations.signature: lemma orderAt_eval_ge (Q : F[X][Y]) (P : F[X]) (x : F) (m : ℕ)

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.roots_le_degree_of_deg_lt_roots
declarations.signature: lemma roots_le_degree_of_deg_lt_roots (R : F[X]) (m : ℕ) (A : Finset (Fin n))
declarations.doc: If a polynomial R has roots at points indexed by A with multiplicity at least m, and its degree is strictly less than m * |A|, then R must be the zero polynomial.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.interpolate_eq_of_degree_lt
declarations.doc: If a polynomial `q` has degree less than `n`, then interpolating its values at `n` points recovers `q`.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.codewordToPoly_degree_le
declarations.signature: lemma codewordToPoly_degree_le (hk : k + 1 ≤ n) (p : code ωs k) :
declarations.doc: The polynomial corresponding to a codeword has degree at most k-1.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.sufficient_multiplicity_bound
declarations.doc: The degree bound is strictly less than `m` times the number of agreement points, provided the distance is within the Johnson radius.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.dvd_property
declarations.signature: theorem dvd_property [DecidableEq F] (hk : k + 1 ≤ n) (hm : 1 ≤ m) (p : code ωs k)
declarations.doc: If $Q$ satisfies the weighted degree bound and vanishes to order $m$ at each point $(\omega_i, f_i)$, and if $P$ is a codeword close enough to $f$, then $Y - P(X)$ divides $Q(X,Y)$.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.gs_degree_bound_sq_gt
declarations.doc: Lower bound: (gs_degree_bound + 1)^2 > (m+1/2)^2 * k * n.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.gs_numVars_gt_numConstraints_of_gt_one
declarations.doc: numVars with gs_degree_bound exceeds numConstraints (for k > 1).

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.gs_sufficient_multiplicity_bound
declarations.doc: The degree bound with ρ = k/n is strictly less than m times the number of agreement points, provided the distance is within the rate-corrected Johnson radius gs_johnson.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.gs_dvd_property
declarations.signature: theorem gs_dvd_property [DecidableEq F] (hk : k + 1 ≤ n) (hm : 1 ≤ m) (p : code ωs k)
declarations.doc: Divisibility via the rate-corrected GS system. Uses gs_degree_bound (ρ=k/n) and gs_johnson instead of the conservative proximity_gap versions.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.Conditions
declarations.signature: structure Conditions (D : ℕ) (ωs : Fin n ↪ F) (f : Fin n → F) (Q : F[X][Y]) where
declarations.doc: Guruswami–Sudan conditions for the polynomial searched by the decoder. These conditions characterize a nonzero bivariate polynomial `Q(X,Y)` with bounded weighted degree that vanishes with sufficiently high multiplicity at all interpolation points `(ωs i, f i)`. As in the Berlekamp–Welch case, finding such a polynomial can be shown to be equivalent to solving a system of linear equations. Here: * `D : ℕ` — the **degree bound** for `Q` under the weighted degree measure. * `ωs : Fin n ↪ F` — the **domain of evaluation**, i.e. the interpolation points. * `f : Fin n → F` — the **received word**. I

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.decoder
declarations.signature: opaque decoder (k r D e : ℕ) (ωs : Fin n ↪ F) (f : Fin n → F) : List F[X] := sorry
declarations.doc: Guruswami-Sudan decoder.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.decoder_mem_impl_dist
declarations.doc: Each decoded codeword has to be e-far from the received message.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.decoder_dist_impl_mem
declarations.doc: If a codeword is e-far from the received message it appears in the output of the decoder.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.proximity_gap_existence
declarations.signature: theorem proximity_gap_existence (k n : ℕ) (ωs : Fin n ↪ F) (f : Fin n → F) (hm : 1 ≤ m) :
declarations.doc: Existence of a solution to the Guruswami-Sudan decoder. It is the first part of Lemma 5.3 from [BCIKS20].

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.proximity_gap_divisibility
declarations.signature: theorem proximity_gap_divisibility (hk : k + 1 ≤ n) (hm : 1 ≤ m) (p : code ωs k)
declarations.doc: Given any Reed-Solomon code `p`, any solution of the Guruswami-Sudan decoder is divisible by `Y - P(X)`, where `P(X)` is the polynomial corresponding to the codeword `p`. It is the first part of Lemma 5.3 from [BCIKS20].

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.gs_existence
declarations.signature: theorem gs_existence (k n : ℕ) (ωs : Fin n ↪ F) (f : Fin n → F)
declarations.doc: GS existence with rate-corrected degree bound (ρ = k/n). Requires k > 1 for the counting argument and m ≥ 1 for multiplicity.

declarations.name: GuruswamiSudan.gs_divisibility
declarations.signature: theorem gs_divisibility (hk : k + 1 ≤ n) (hm : 1 ≤ m) (p : code ωs k)
declarations.doc: GS divisibility with rate-corrected Johnson radius (ρ = k/n).

declarations.name: CodingTheory.hammingBallVolume
declarations.signature: def hammingBallVolume (q : ℕ) (δ : ℝ) (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Definition 2.4.** Volume of the Hamming ball of relative radius `δ` over an alphabet of size `q` and block length `n`: `Vol_q(δ, n) := ∑_{i=0}^{⌊δ · n⌋} (n choose i) · (q-1)^i`. Counts the number of words in `Σ^n` (with `|Σ| = q`) within absolute Hamming distance `⌊δ · n⌋` of any fixed center. Independent of the choice of center. Used in `ABF26-L3.7` (Elias lower bound) and `ABF26-C3.8` (volume-based lower bound). Noncomputable because the floor `⌊δ · n⌋₊` over `ℝ` is noncomputable (Mathlib's `Nat.floor` on `ℝ` depends on a `noncomputable` `linearOrder` instance).

declarations.name: CodingTheory.hammingBallVolume_zero_radius
declarations.signature: lemma hammingBallVolume_zero_radius (q n : ℕ) : hammingBallVolume q 0 n = 1 := by

declarations.name: CodingTheory.card_filter_hammingDist_eq
declarations.doc: **Key combinatorial identity.** The number of vectors `x : ι → F` at Hamming distance exactly `i` from a fixed `y` is `C(n, i) · (q-1)^i`, where `n = |ι|` and `q = |F|`. Independent of `y`. Proof via an explicit bijection: `x` corresponds to the pair `(S, f)` where `S := {j | x j ≠ y j}` (an `i`-element subset of `ι`) and `f : S → F` is the restriction of `x` to `S` (each value forced into `F \ {y j}`). Counting: `Σ S ∈ powersetCard i univ, ∏ j ∈ S, (|F| - 1) = C(n, i) · (q-1)^i`.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.hammingBallVolume_eq_ncard_hammingBall
declarations.doc: **Bridge to `hammingBall`.** The volume function counts the cardinality of the existing `hammingBall` (set of words within radius `⌊δ·n⌋` of any fixed center). The identity collapses to the standard combinatorial fact `#{x ∈ F^n : Δ(x, y) ≤ r} = ∑_{i ≤ r} C(n, i) · (q-1)^i` independent of `y`. This links the ArkLib `ListDecodability.hammingBall` (set-form, used elsewhere in the list-decoding development) to ABF26 Definition 2.4's `Vol_q(δ, n)` (cardinality form). Proof: partition `hammingBall y r` by exact distance via `card_filter_hammingDist_eq`, then sum.

declarations.name: InterleavedCode.lambda_le_ggr11
declarations.signature: theorem lambda_le_ggr11 {ι F : Type} [Fintype ι] [Field F] [DecidableEq F]
declarations.doc: **Lemma 2.10 of [ABF26]** (= **[GGR11]**) — interleaved-code list-size bound. Let `C` be a code with relative minimum distance `δ_C := δ_min(C) / |ι|`, and let `δ ∈ [0, δ_C)`. Define `η := δ_C - δ`, `b := ⌈δ / η⌉`, `r := ⌈log₂(δ_C / η)⌉`. Then for every `m ≥ 1`, `|Λ(C^{≡m}, δ)| ≤ (b+r choose r) · |Λ(C, δ)|^r`. The key feature is that the bound's dependence on the interleaving factor `m` is hidden inside the constant `(b+r choose r)` — once `δ` is fixed, the list size of `C^{≡m}` grows as a *polynomial in* `|Λ(C, δ)|` of degree `r`, **independent of `m`**. Used in ABF26 §3 list-decoding analyse

declarations.name: JohnsonBound.Jqℓ
declarations.signature: def Jqℓ (q ℓ : ℚ) (δ : ℚ) : ℝ :=
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Definition 3.1, `J_{q,ℓ}`.** Paper's q-ary ℓ-radius Johnson function: `J_{q,ℓ}(δ) := (1 - 1/q) · (1 - √(1 - q/(q-1) · ℓ/(ℓ-1) · δ))` For `ℓ = 2` this is the binary Johnson radius; as `ℓ → ∞`, `Jqℓ q ℓ δ → J q δ` (the existing `JohnsonBound.J`). The `ℓ` parameter is the target list size.

declarations.name: JohnsonBound.Jcap
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Definition 3.1, `J`.** Paper's asymptotic Johnson bound: `J(δ) := 1 - √(1 - δ)` Equals the `q → ∞` limit of `J_q(δ)` and the `q, ℓ → ∞` limit of `J_{q,ℓ}(δ)`. This is also the binary Johnson bound (q = 2, ℓ → ∞). Distinct from the existing `JohnsonBound.J q δ`, which is the paper's `J_q(δ)` (the q-ary limit, parametrised by `q`). To avoid renaming the existing `J`, we name this `Jcap` (Johnson — *cap*acity).

declarations.name: CodingTheory.johnson_bound_lambda_le_ell
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 3.2 [Joh62].** Johnson bound on list size. For any code `C ⊆ Σ^n` with `|Σ| = q`, `|Λ(C, J_{q,ℓ}(δ_min(C)))| ≤ ℓ` where `δ_min(C) = minDist(C) / n` is the relative minimum distance and `J_{q,ℓ}` is the paper's q-ary ℓ-radius Johnson function. An existing in-tree proof of the Johnson bound in absolute-distance form lives in [`JohnsonBound/Basic.lean`](Basic.lean) (`johnson_bound`, `johnson_bound_alphabet_free`); porting it to this ABF26-style `Lambda`-based form is tracked in `ABF26_PLAN.md`. **Alphabet generality.** Stated over an arbitrary alphabet `α` (not necessarily a field

declarations.name: CodingTheory.mds_johnson_lambda_le
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Corollary 3.3.** MDS coarse Johnson corollary. For every MDS code `C` with rate `ρ := dim C / n` and `η > 0`: `|Λ(C, 1 - √ρ - η)| ≤ 1 / (2 · η · ρ)` Derives from L2.6 (Singleton bound: MDS implies `δ_min = 1 - ρ + 1/n`, available via the `IsMDS_iff_rate_distance` bridge) plus T3.2 (or its asymptotic version via `Jcap`). Admitted as an external result; the path to a machine-checked proof requires the asymptotic-Johnson form `Lambda C δ ≤ 1/(2·(Jcap δ - δ))` plus MDS rate-distance manipulation. **Rate derivation.** `ρ` is bound inline as `(Module.finrank F C : ℝ) / Fintype.card ι` rather

declarations.name: ListDecodable.Code.
declarations.signature: abbrev Code. {u, v} (ι : Type u) (S : Type v) : Type (max u v) := Set (ι → S)

declarations.name: ListDecodable.hammingBall
declarations.signature: def hammingBall (y : ι → F) (r : ℕ) : Set (ι → F) :=
declarations.doc: Hamming ball of radius `r` centred at a word `y`.

declarations.name: ListDecodable.relHammingBall
declarations.signature: def relHammingBall (y : ι → F) (r : ℝ) : Set (ι → F) :=
declarations.doc: Ball of radius `r` centred at a word `y` with respect to the relative Hamming distance.

declarations.name: ListDecodable.closeCodewords
declarations.signature: def closeCodewords (C : Code ι F) (y : ι → F) (r : ℕ) : Set (ι → F) :=
declarations.doc: The set of `r`-close codewords to a given word `y` with respect to the Hamming distance.

declarations.name: ListDecodable.closeCodewordsRel
declarations.signature: def closeCodewordsRel (C : Code ι F) (y : ι → F) (r : ℝ) : Set (ι → F) :=
declarations.doc: The set of `r`-close codewords to a given word `y` with respect to the relative Hamming distance. Note that this is exactly `Λ (C, y, r)` from [ACFY24] and ` List (C, y, r)` from [ACFY24stir].

declarations.name: ListDecodable.listDecodable
declarations.signature: def listDecodable (C : Code ι F) (r : ℝ) (ℓ : ℝ) : Prop :=
declarations.doc: A code `C` is `(r,ℓ)`-list decodable. - Remark: Note that the number of codewords `ℓ` in the Hamming ball of radius `r` centred around `y` is a real number. The reasoning for this is to accommodate the statement of the Johnson Bound Theorem. For simplicity and ease of proving statements, `ℓ` can be considered a a natural number by taking the floor of the real value. This will not lead to information loss since the cardinality of the set of close codewords is a natural number anyway.

declarations.name: ListDecodable.uniqueDecodable
declarations.signature: def uniqueDecodable (C : Code ι F) (r : ℝ) : Prop :=
declarations.doc: A code `C` is uniquely decodable up to a relative distance `r` if for any word `y : ι → F`, there is at most one codeword in `C` within a relative Hamming distance of `r`. This is a special case of list decodability where the list size `ℓ` is `1`.

declarations.name: ListDecodable.Lambda_at
declarations.signature: abbrev Lambda_at (C : Code ι F) (δ : ℝ) (f : ι → F) : Set (ι → F) :=
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Definition 2.8 (point list).** The set of codewords of `C` within relative Hamming distance `δ` of `f`. Synonymous with `closeCodewordsRel`; named to match the paper's `Λ(C, δ, f)`.

declarations.name: ListDecodable.Lambda
declarations.signature: def Lambda (C : Code ι F) (δ : ℝ) : ℕ∞ :=
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Definition 2.8 (maximised list size).** The maximum over words `f` of `|Λ(C, δ, f)|`. Named to match the paper's `|Λ(C, δ)|`.

declarations.name: ListDecodable.Lambda_at_subset_of_le
declarations.signature: lemma Lambda_at_subset_of_le {C : Code ι F} {δ₁ δ₂ : ℝ}
declarations.doc: `Lambda_at` is monotone in the radius.

declarations.name: ListDecodable.Lambda_mono
declarations.signature: lemma Lambda_mono {C : Code ι F} {δ₁ δ₂ : ℝ} [Finite F] (h : δ₁ ≤ δ₂) :
declarations.doc: `Lambda` is monotone in the radius.

declarations.name: ListDecodable.Lambda_at_subset_code
declarations.signature: lemma Lambda_at_subset_code {C : Code ι F} (δ : ℝ) (f : ι → F) :
declarations.doc: Any element of `Lambda_at C δ f` is a codeword of `C`.

declarations.name: ListDecodable.ncard_Lambda_at_le_ncard
declarations.signature: lemma ncard_Lambda_at_le_ncard {C : Code ι F} (δ : ℝ) (f : ι → F) (hC : C.Finite) :
declarations.doc: `|Λ(C, δ, f)| ≤ |C|` for finite `C`.

declarations.name: ListDecodable.Lambda_le_ncard

declarations.name: _anon_theorem_35
declarations.signature: theorem theorem's outermost binder. The user instantiates at the call site.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.linear_lambda_ge_elias_volume_eli57
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Lemma 3.7 [Eli57].** Elias volume lower bound on list size: `|Λ(C, δ)| ≥ Vol_q(δ, n) / q^(n-k)` where `q = |F|`, `n = |ι|`, and `k = dim(C)` is the dimension of the linear code `C` (so `|C| = q^k`). The paper's proof uses an averaging argument over random words; we admit it here as an external result. Uses `hammingBallVolume` (ABF26 D2.4) from `HammingBallVolume.lean`.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.linear_lambda_ge_entropy_volume
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Corollary 3.8.** Volume-based lower bound on list size, using the MS77 volume estimate `Vol_q(δ, n) ≥ q^{n·(ρ-1+H_q(δ))} / √(8·n·δ·(1-δ))`. With `ρ := k/n`: `|Λ(C, δ)| ≥ q^{n·(ρ - 1 + H_q(δ))} / √(8·n·δ·(1-δ))` Uses `qEntropy` (ABF26 D2.2). Admitted as an external result.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.linear_C_le_generalized_singleton_st20
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 3.9 [ST20 Thm 1.2].** Generalized Singleton bound for list decoding. Let `F` be a finite field, `0 < ℓ < |F|`, `δ ∈ (0, 1)`, and let `C ⊆ F^n` be a linear error-correcting code of rate `ρ` with `|Λ(C, δ)| ≤ ℓ`. Then: `|C| ≤ |F|^{n - ⌊(ℓ+1)/ℓ · δ · n⌋}` Equivalently, `δ ≤ ℓ/(ℓ+1) · (1-ρ)`. Admitted as an external result.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.large_alphabet_barrier_bdg24_agl23
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 3.10 [BDG24, AGL23].** Large-alphabet barrier for generalized Singleton attainment. For every `ℓ ≥ 2` and `ρ ∈ (0, 1)` there exists a constant `α_ℓρ > 0` such that for every `η > 0` and every sufficiently large `n`, every error- correcting code `C ⊆ F^n` of rate `ρ` with `|Λ(C, ℓ/(ℓ+1) · (1-ρ-η))| ≤ ℓ` satisfies: `|F| ≥ 2^{α_ℓρ / η}` i.e. attaining the generalized Singleton bound up to `η` slack requires alphabet size exponential in `1/η`. We existentially package the "sufficiently large" threshold as an explicit `n₀` parameter rather than relying on Lean's `eventually` API. Ad

declarations.name: CodingTheory.random_linear_lambda_lower_glmrsw22
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 3.11 [GLMRSW22 Thm 4.1].** Random linear code lower bound. Fix a prime `q`, `δ ∈ (0, 1 - 1/q)`, and `ε ∈ (0, 1)`. There exists `γ > 0` such that for all `1 - H_q(δ) - γ < ρ < 1 - H_q(δ)` and all sufficiently large `n`, some linear code `C ⊆ F^n` of rate `ρ` satisfies: `|Λ(C, δ)| > ⌊H_q(δ) / (1 - H_q(δ) - ρ) - ε⌋` The paper's full statement gives a `1 - q^{-Ω(n)}` probability over the choice of `C`; we existentially package this as "there exists a witness code" since ArkLib does not yet have a probability distribution over linear codes.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.rs_lambda_superpoly_extension_bkr06
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 3.12 [BKR06 Cor 2.2].** Reed-Solomon superpolynomial list-size over extension fields. Fix `0 < α < β < 1`. For infinitely many prime powers `q` there exists a Reed-Solomon code `C := RS[F_q, F_q, ⌊q^α⌋]` and a word `w : F_q → F_q` such that: `|Λ(C, 1 - q^{β-1}, w)| ≥ q^{(α - β²) · log q}` Admitted as an external result.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.rs_lambda_large_prime_ghsz02
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 3.13 [GHSZ02 Cor 20].** Reed-Solomon large list-size over prime fields. Fix `0 < α, β < 1`. For all sufficiently large primes `p`, there exists `C := RS[F_p, F_p, ⌊p^α⌋]` and a word `w : F_p → F_p` such that: `|Λ(C, 1 - ((1-β)/α) · p^{α-1}, w)| > Ω(p^{p^α · β/2})` Admitted as an external result.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.rs_lambda_high_rate_jh01
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 3.14 [JH01 Thm 2].** Large-rate Reed-Solomon lower bound. Fix an integer `j ≥ 2`. For infinitely many prime powers `q` with `q ≡ 1 (mod j+1)`, there exists `C := RS[F_q, L, k]` with `|C| = j + 1` and rate `ρ ≈ (j-1)/(j+1)` together with a word `w : L → F_q` such that: `|Λ(C, 1/(j+1), w)| > j` Witnesses that high-rate RS codes cannot be list-decoded beyond `1/(j+1)` with list size `j`. Admitted as an external result.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.subspaceDesign_list_decoding_cz25
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 3.4 [CZ25 Theorem B.5].** τ-subspace-design codes are list-decodable up to capacity. Let `C : F^k → (F^s)^n` be a τ-subspace-design code. For every `η > 0`: `|Λ(C, 1 - τ(1/η) - η)| ≤ (1 - τ(1/η)) / η` Combined with `IsSubspaceDesign` (ABF26 D2.16) and `subspaceDesign_tau_lower` (L2.17), this gives a list-decoding bound up to capacity for any subspace-design code. Admitted as an external result.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.frs_list_decoding_capacity_cz25
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Corollary 3.5 [CZ25 Corollary 2.21].** Folded Reed-Solomon codes are list-decodable up to capacity. Let `C := FRS[F, L, k, s, ω]` be a folded RS code of rate `ρ`. For any `η > 0` with `1/η < s`: `|Λ(C, 1 - ρ·s/(s - 1/η + 1) - η)| ≤ (s·(1-ρ) + 1 - 1/η) / (η·(s + 1 - 1/η))` When `η ≥ √(3/s)`, the bound simplifies to `|Λ(C, 1 - ρ - η)| ≤ 1/η`. Derives from T3.4 + T2.18 (FRS is τ-subspace-design). Admitted as an external result.

declarations.name: ProximityToRS.e_le_dist_over_3_strong
declarations.doc: **Lemma 4.4, [AHIV22] (strong form).** Either all points on the affine line are `e`-close to the Reed–Solomon code, or at most `‖RS‖₀` points are.

declarations.name: ProximityToRS.dir_close_of_many_close_pts
declarations.doc: If an affine line has too many `e`-close points to the Reed–Solomon code, then its direction is itself `e`-close to the code.

declarations.name: ProximityToRS.all_close_not_few_close_pts
declarations.doc: If every point on a nondegenerate affine line is close and the field is larger than the Reed-Solomon minimum distance, then the line cannot have only few close points.

declarations.name: ProximityToRS.e_le_dist_over_3
declarations.doc: **Lemma 4.4, [AHIV22] (mutual-exclusion corollary).** Either all points on the affine line are `e`-close to the Reed–Solomon code, or at most `‖RS‖₀` points are. The assumptions `v ≠ 0` and `‖RS‖₀ < |F|` are necessary for mutual exclusion: if `v = 0`, the affine line degenerates to a singleton and the two branches can hold simultaneously.

declarations.name: ProximityToRS.prob_of_bad_pts
declarations.doc: **Lemma 4.5, [AHIV22].** If the interleaved word `U⋆` is far from the interleaved Reed–Solomon code, then a uniformly random word in the row-span is `e`-close to the code with probability at most `‖RS‖₀ / |F|`.

declarations.name: vecSupport
declarations.signature: def vecSupport (u : ι → F) : Finset ι :=
declarations.doc: The finite support of a vector over `F`.

declarations.name: mem_vecSupport
declarations.doc: Membership in `vecSupport` is exactly nonvanishing at that coordinate.

declarations.name: not_mem_vecSupport
declarations.doc: Non-membership in `vecSupport` is exactly vanishing at that coordinate.

declarations.name: vecSupport_sub
declarations.doc: The support of a difference is the set of coordinates where the two words disagree.

declarations.name: hamming_dist_le_of_subset_disagree
declarations.doc: If every coordinate where two words disagree lies in `D`, then their Hamming distance is at most `D.card`.

declarations.name: exists_common_support_of_wt_le
declarations.doc: A finite-dimensional submodule whose vectors all have support size at most `e` over a field with more than `e` elements has a common support of size at most `e`.

declarations.name: dist_interleaved_code_to_code_lb
declarations.doc: **Lemma 4.3, [AHIV22]** (row-span lower bound). If the interleaved word `U⋆` is more than `e` far from the interleaved code `L^⋈κ`, then the row-span of `U⋆` contains a word more than `e` far from `L`. The additional field-size assumption `|F| > e` is needed: for small fields one can have a linear subspace of `F^ι` consisting entirely of `e`-sparse vectors whose supports are not aligned, making `⋈|U⋆` column-wise far while every row-span vector stays `e`-close.

declarations.name: ProximityToRS.closePtsOnAffineLine

declarations.doc: Theorem 1.4 (Main Theorem — Correlated agreement over lines) in [BCIKS20]. Take a Reed-Solomon code of length `ι` and degree `deg`, a proximity-error parameter pair `(δ, ε)` and two words `u₀` and `u₁`, such that the probability that a random affine line passing through `u₀` and `u₁` is `δ`-close to Reed-Solomon code is at most `ε`. Then, the words `u₀` and `u₁` have correlated agreement.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.jointAgreement_implies_linSpan_proximity
declarations.doc: Generalisation of `jointAgreement_implies_second_proximity` to an arbitrary word stack over a submodule code. If a stack `W : Fin k → ι → F` jointly agrees with a submodule `C ⊆ ι → F`, then every element of the linear span of the stack is `δ`-close to `C`. The pointwise case `W i ∈ C` is the special case `x = W i` (choose coefficients `c` to be the i-th basis vector); the original `Fin 2` lemma is the case `k = 2`, `x = W 1`. The proof bounds the linear combination against the matching linear combination of the agreement witnesses: on each agreement column `j ∈ S`, `v i j = W i j` for every `

declarations.name: ProximityGap.affine_mem_iff_finset_mem
declarations.doc: The AffineSubspace and Finset.image representations of an affine subspace have the same membership.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.prob_coeff_eq_prob_affine
declarations.doc: The coefficient-parameterised probability equals the subtype probability. The map `r ↦ u₀ + ∑ rᵢ • dᵢ` has constant-cardinality fibers (cosets of the kernel of the linear part), so pushforward of uniform gives uniform.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.hammingDist_smul_eq
declarations.signature: theorem hammingDist_smul_eq {z : F} (hz : z ≠ 0) (u v : ι → F) :
declarations.doc: Hamming distance is invariant under scaling by a unit: `hammingDist (z • u) (z • v) = hammingDist u v` for `z ≠ 0`.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.relHammingDist_smul_eq
declarations.signature: theorem relHammingDist_smul_eq {z : F} (hz : z ≠ 0) (u v : ι → F) :
declarations.doc: Relative Hamming distance is invariant under scaling by a unit.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.relDistFromCode_smul_eq
declarations.doc: Relative distance to a submodule is invariant under scaling by a unit: `δᵣ(z • u, V) = δᵣ(u, V)` for `z ≠ 0` and `V` a submodule. Key step in BCIKS20 §6.3 (Step 1c).

declarations.name: ProximityGap.spanU_eq_sup
declarations.signature: lemma spanU_eq_sup {k : ℕ} (u : Fin (k + 1) → ι → F)
declarations.doc: When `u₀ ∉ U'`, `span(range u) = span {u₀} ⊔ U'`.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.mem_spanU_decomp
declarations.signature: lemma mem_spanU_decomp {k : ℕ} (u : Fin (k + 1) → ι → F)
declarations.doc: Every element of `span(range u)` decomposes as `c • u₀ + d` with `d ∈ U'`.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.coset_scalar_eq
declarations.signature: lemma coset_scalar_eq {u₀ : ι → F} {U' : Submodule F (ι → F)}
declarations.doc: If `u₀ ∉ U'` and `a • u₀ + d₁ = b • u₀ + d₂` with `d₁ d₂ ∈ U'`, then `a = b`.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.all_affine_elements_close
declarations.doc: Every element of an affine subspace U is δ-close to a RS code V, given Pr_{x∈U}[δᵣ(x,V) ≤ δ] > ε (BCIKS20 §6.3, Step 1). Proof strategy: 1. Apply Lemma 6.3 to U → all directions in U' are δ-close to V. 2. Scaling invariance: δᵣ(z·x, V) = δᵣ(x, V) for z ≠ 0, V a submodule. 3. Probability transfer: Pr[close on span(U)] > ε. Key: all |U'| direction elements are close (step 1) + scaling gives Pr_Ū ≥ 1/|F| + (1-1/|F|)·Pr_U > ε since ε < 1. 4. Apply Lemma 6.3 to span(U) → all elements of span(U) are close. Since U ⊆ span(U), all elements of U are close.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.bucket_exists_common_codeword
declarations.doc: BCIKS20 §6.3 bucketing: given an affine subspace U whose elements are all δ-close to a linear code V, there exist a codeword v₀ and agreement set D' of size ≥ (1-δ)|ι| such that the basepoint agrees with v₀ on D' and every generator direction agrees with some codeword on D'.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.exists_large_of_finset_cover
declarations.doc: Pigeonhole for finite covers: if `U` is covered by `L` indexed subsets and `L * B < |U|`, then some subset has more than `B` elements.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.card_roots_finset_le_natDegree
declarations.doc: If `S` is a finite set of elements that are all roots of a nonzero polynomial `Q`, then `|S| ≤ deg(Q)`. Wrapper around Mathlib's `card_le_degree_of_subset_roots`.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.card_divisors_lt_field
declarations.doc: The Guruswami-Sudan list-decoding bound: given a nonzero polynomial `Q` over `F[X]` whose `Y`-degree is less than `|F|`, the number of distinct polynomials `P` such that `(Y - P(X)) | Q(X, Y)` is strictly less than `|F|`. This is the structural core of the list-decoding argument (BCIKS20 §5).

declarations.name: ProximityGap.gs_degree_bound_le_inv_mu
declarations.doc: Degree-bound numerator step: `(m + 1/2) * s * n / (deg - 1) ≤ 5 / (4 * μ)`. Extracted from `exists_gs_multiplicity` to reduce heartbeat pressure.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.exists_gs_multiplicity
declarations.signature: lemma exists_gs_multiplicity {deg : ℕ} {domain : ι ↪ F} {δ : ℝ≥0}
declarations.doc: Construct a GS multiplicity `m` satisfying both the Johnson radius bound and the degree bound. Witness: `m = ⌈√ρ/(2η)⌉ + 1` where `η = 1 - √ρ - δ`.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.correlatedAgreement_affine_spaces
declarations.doc: Theorem 1.7 (Correlated agreement over affine spaces) in [BCIKS20]. Take a Reed-Solomon code of length `ι` and degree `deg`, a proximity-error parameter pair `(δ, ε)` and an affine space with origin `u₀` and affine generating set `u₁, ..., uκ` such that the probability a random point in the affine space is `δ`-close to the Reed-Solomon code is greater than `ε`. Then the words `u₀, ..., uκ` have correlated agreement. Note that we have `k + 2` vectors to form the affine space. This an intricacy needed us to be able to isolate the affine origin from the affine span and to form a generating set of

declarations.name: ProximityGap.correlatedAgreement_affine_curves
declarations.doc: Theorem 1.5 (Correlated agreement for low-degree parameterised curves) in [BCIKS20]. Take a Reed-Solomon code of length `ι` and degree `deg`, a proximity-error parameter pair `(δ, ε)` and a curve passing through words `u₀, ..., uκ`, such that the probability that a random point on the curve is `δ`-close to the Reed-Solomon code is at most `ε`. Then, the words `u₀, ..., uκ` have correlated agreement.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.coeffs_of_close_proximity_curve
declarations.doc: The parameters for which the curve points are `δ`-close to a set `V` (typically, a linear code). This is the set `S` from the proximity gap paper.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.large_agreement_set_on_curve_implies_correlated_agreement
declarations.doc: If the set of points `δ`-close to the code `V` has at least `n * l + 1` points, then there exists a curve defined by vectors `v` from `V` such that the points of `curve u` and `curve v` are `δ`-close with the same parameters. Moreover, `u` and `v` differ at at most `δ * n` positions.

declarations.name: ProximityGap._anon_def_72
declarations.signature: def noncomputable def δ₀ (rho : ℚ) (m : ℕ) : ℝ :=
declarations.doc: The distance bound from [BCIKS20].

declarations.name: ProximityGap.large_agreement_set_on_curve_implies_correlated_agreement'
declarations.doc: If the set of points on the curve defined by `u` close to `V` has at least `((1 + 1 / (2 * m)) ^ 7 * m ^ 7) / (3 * (Real.rpow rho (3 / 2 : ℚ))) * n ^ 2 * l + 1` points, then there exist vectors `v` from `V` that are `(1 - δ) * n` close to `u`.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.errorBound
declarations.signature: def errorBound (δ : ℝ≥0) (deg : ℕ) (domain : ι ↪ F) : ℝ≥0 :=
declarations.doc: The error bound `ε` in the pair of proximity and error parameters `(δ,ε)` for Reed-Solomon codes defined up to the Johnson bound. More precisely, let `ρ` be the rate of the Reed-Solomon code. Then for `δ ∈ (0, 1 - √ρ)`, we define the relevant error parameter `ε` for the unique decoding bound, i.e. `δ ∈ (0, (1-ρ)/2]` and Johnson bound, i.e. `δ ∈ ((1-ρ)/2 , 1 - √ρ)`. Otherwise, we set `ε = 0`.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.proximity_gap_RSCodes
declarations.signature: theorem proximity_gap_RSCodes {k t : ℕ} [NeZero k] [NeZero t] {deg : ℕ} {domain : ι ↪ F}
declarations.doc: Theorem 1.2 (Proximity Gaps for Reed-Solomon codes) in [BCIKS20]. Let `C` be a collection of affine spaces. Then `C` displays a `(δ, ε)`-proximity gap with respect to a Reed-Solomon code, where `(δ, ε)` are the proximity and error parameters defined up to the Johnson bound. The `hε : errorBound δ deg domain < 1` hypothesis is required for the `Xor'` exclusivity in `δ_ε_proximityGap`: without `ε < 1`, the two branches `Pr = 1` and `Pr ≤ ε` could both hold when `ε = 1`, violating `Xor'`. This proof depends on `correlatedAgreement_affine_spaces` (Theorem 1.7) in `AffineSpaces.lean`.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.WeightedAgreement.weighted_correlated_agreement_for_parameterized_curves
declarations.doc: Weighted correlated agreement over curves. Take a Reed-Solomon code of length `ι` and degree `deg`, a proximity-error parameter pair `(δ, ε)` and a curve generated by vectors `u`, such that the probability that a random point on the curve is `δ`-close to Reed-Solomon code is at most `ε`. Then, the words `u` have weighted correlated agreement.

We are given two lists of functions `u, v : Fin (l + 2) → ι → F`, where each `v i` is a Reed-Solomon codeword of degree `deg` over the evaluation domain `domain`. From these lists we form the bivariate curve evaluations * `w x z = Curve.polynomialCurveEval u z x`, * `wtilde x z = Curve.polynomialCurveEval v z x`. Fix a finite set `S' ⊆ F` with `S'.card > l + 1`, and a (product) measure `μ` on the evaluation domain `ι`. Assume that for every `z ∈ S'` the one-dimensional functions `w · z` and `wti

declarations.name: ProximityGap.WeightedAgreement.sufficiently_large_list_agreement_on_curve_implies_correlated_agreement
declarations.doc: Lemma 7.6 in [BCIKS20]. This is the "integral-weight" strengthening of the list-agreement-on-a-curve => correlated-agreement bound. We have two lists of functions `u, v : Fin (l + 2) → ι → F`, where each `v i` is a Reed-Solomon codeword of degree `deg` over the evaluation domain `domain`. From these lists we form the bivariate curve evaluations * `w x z = Curve.polynomialCurveEval u z x`, * `wtilde x z = Curve.polynomialCurveEval v z x`. The domain `ι` is finite and is equipped with a weighted measure `μ`, where each weight `μ i` is a rational with common denominator `M`. Let `S' ⊆ F` be a set

declarations.name: ProximityGap.proximityMeasure
declarations.signature: def proximityMeasure (u v : Word A ι) (d : ℕ) : ℕ :=
declarations.doc: The proximity measure of two vectors `u` and `v` from a code `C` at distance `d` is the number of vectors at distance at most `d` from the linear combination of `u` and `v` with coefficients `r` in `F`.

declarations.name: ProximityGap.proximityGap
declarations.doc: A code `C` exhibits proximity gap at distance `d` and cardinality bound `bound` if for every pair of vectors `u` and `v`, whenever the proximity measure for `C u v d` is greater than `bound`, then the distance of `[u | v]` from the interleaved code `C ^⊗ 2` is at most `d`.

declarations.name: ProximityGap._anon_def_99
declarations.signature: def noncomputable def δ_ε_proximityGap {α : Type} [DecidableEq α] [Nonempty α]
declarations.doc: Definition 1.1 in [BCIKS20]. Let `P` be a set `P` and `C` a collection of sets. We say that `C` displays a `(δ, ε)`-proximity gap with respect to `P` and the relative Hamming distance measure if for every `S ∈ C` exactly one of the following holds: 1. The probability that a randomly sampled element `s` from `S` is `δ`-close to `P` is `1`. 2. The probability that a randomly sampled element `s` from `S` is `δ`-close to `P` is at most `ε`. We call `δ` the proximity parameter and `ε` the error parameter.

declarations.name: ProximityGap._anon_def_111
declarations.signature: def noncomputable def δ_ε_correlatedAgreementAffineLines [Module F A]
declarations.doc: Definition: `(δ, ε)`-correlated agreement for affine lines. For every pair of words `u₀, u₁`, if the probability that a random affine line `u₀ + z • u₁` is `δ`-close to `C` exceeds `ε`, then `u₀` and `u₁` have correlated agreement with `C`. -- **TODO**: prove that `δ_ε_correlatedAgreementAffineLines` implies `δ_ε_proximityGap`

declarations.name: ProximityGap._anon_def_122
declarations.signature: def def δ_ε_multilinearCorrelatedAgreement [CommRing F] [Module F A]
declarations.doc: **[Definition 2.3, DG25]** We say that `C ⊂ F^n` features multilinear correlated agreement with respect to the proximity parameter `δ` and the error bound `ε`, folding degree `ϑ > 0` if: ∀ word stack `u` of size `2^ϑ`, if the probability that (a random multilinear combination of the word stack `u` with randomness `r` is `δ`-close to `C`) exceeds `ε`, then the word stack `u` has correlated agreement with `C ^⋈ (2^ϑ)`.

declarations.name: ProximityGap._anon_def_141
declarations.doc: **`(δ, ε)`-CA for low-degree parameterised (polynomial) curves**: Generalized statement of **Theorem 1.5, [BCIKS20]** For `k+1` words `u₀, u₁, ..., uₖ ∈ A^ι` let `curve(u) = {∑_{i ∈ {0, ..., k}}, z^i • u_i | z ∈ 𝔽}` be a low-degree parameterised polynomial curve. If the probability that a random point in `curve(u)` is `δ`-close to `C` exceeds `k * ε` (not `(k+1) * ε`), then the words `u₀, ..., uₖ` have correlated agreement. **NOTE**: this definition could be converted into the form of Pr_{let r ← $ᵖ F}[...] if we want: + consistency with `δ_ε_correlatedAgreementAffineLines` + making `A` be of

declarations.name: ProximityGap._anon_def_157
declarations.doc: **`(δ, ε)`-CA for affine spaces**: Generalized statement of **Theorem 1.6, [BCIKS20]** For `k+1` words `u₀, u₁, ..., uₖ ∈ A^ι` let `U = u₀ + span{u₁, ..., uₖ} ⊂ A^ι` be an affine subspace (note that `span` here means linear span, so this formulation is not same as the default affine span/affine hull). If the probability that a random point in `U` is `δ`-close to `C` exceeds `ε`, then the words `u₀, u₁, ..., uₖ` have correlated agreement. This samples uniformly from the generated affine subspace. An equivalent coefficient-sampling formulation would sample `r : Fin k → F` and test `u₀ + ∑ i, r i

declarations.name: CodingTheory.linear_epsMCA_1_5_johnson_gkl24
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 4.11, Item 1 [GKL24 Thm 3].** For any linear error-correcting code `C ⊆ F^n`, parameter `η > 0`, and `δ ≤ 1 - ∛(1 - δ_min(C) + η)`: `ε_mca(C, δ) ≤ ((n+6)/η + 2 / (η · (∛(1 - δ_min + η) - √(1 - δ_min + η))) ) · (1/|F|)` The "1.5-Johnson regime" refers to the fact that `1 - ∛(1 - δ_min)` lies strictly above the classical Johnson bound `1 - √(1 - δ_min)` and strictly below capacity. The bound is admitted from the cited paper. **Implicit hypothesis `η < δ_min`.** For the bound's denominator `∛x − √x` (with `x := 1 - δ_min + η`) to be strictly positive we need `x < 1`, i.e. `η < δ_m

declarations.name: CodingTheory.linear_epsCA_1_5_johnson_bgks20
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 4.11, Item 2 [BGKS20 Lem 3.2].** For any linear error-correcting code `C ⊆ F^n`, parameter `η > 0`, and `δ ≤ 1 - ∛(1 - δ_min(C) + η)`: `ε_ca(C, δ_fld := δ, δ_int := δ + η) ≤ 2 / (η² · |F|)` Same regime as the GKL24 form but stated in CA-with-proximity-loss shape. Tighter when the GKL24 bound is dominated by its second term. Admitted from the cited paper. The regime hypothesis `η < δ_min` is shared with Item 1 (the paper presents both bounds under one regime statement); included here for hypothesis-parity even though Item 2's RHS `2 / (η² |F|)` is well-defined for any `η > 0`.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.rs_epsCA_bchks25_item2
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 4.9 Item 2 [BCHKS25 Theorem 1.3].** Reed-Solomon CA bound in the `δ_min/3`-to-Johnson regime. Let `C := RS[F, L, k]` with rate `ρ`. For `δ_min(C)/3 ≤ δ_fld < δ_int`: `ε_ca(C, δ_fld, δ_int) ≤` `  max{ (1-ρ-δ_fld) / (δ_fld·(1-ρ-2·δ_fld)·|F|), δ_int / ((δ_int-δ_fld)·|F|) }` Tighter than T4.8 (AHIV17) in the regime `δ_fld ≥ δ_min/3`. Admitted as an external result.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.rs_epsCA_small_loss_r4_10
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Remark 4.10.** Small-proximity-loss simplification of T4.9.2 via R4.2. For `δ_int - δ_fld = γ/n` with `γ ∈ (0, 1)` (so that `R4.2` collapses `ε_ca` to its `δ_int := δ_fld` value): `ε_mca(C, δ_fld) = ε_ca(C, δ_fld) = ε_ca(C, δ_fld, δ_fld + γ/n) ≤` `  max{ (1-ρ-δ_fld) / (δ_fld·(1-ρ-2·δ_fld)·|F|), (n·δ_fld + γ) / (γ·|F|) }` The `(n·δ_fld + γ) / γ` term dominates the original `δ_int / (δ_int - δ_fld)` term once `δ_int - δ_fld` is below `1/n`. We state the resulting bound on `ε_ca(C, δ_fld, δ_fld)`; the equality with `ε_mca` follows from L4.6 in the unique-decoding regime, which is itself a

declarations.name: CodingTheory.rs_epsMCA_johnson_range_bchks25
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 4.12 [BCHKS25 Thm 4.6].** For `C := RS[F, L, k]` with rate `ρ` and `η > 0`, letting `ρ_plus := ρ + 1/n` and `m := max(⌈√ρ_plus/(2η)⌉, 3)`, for `δ < 1 - √ρ_plus - η`: `ε_mca(C, δ) ≤ (1/|F|) · ( (2(m+½)⁵ + 3(m+½)·δ·ρ_plus) / (3·ρ_plus^{3/2}) · n + (m+½)/√ρ_plus )` The full numeric expression is preserved verbatim so future RS analyses can plug in concrete `ρ`, `η`, and `n` values. Admitted as an external result. **Parameter improvement reference.** ABF26 cites [Hab25] alongside [BCHKS25] for this theorem; Haböck 2025 improves the constants / parameter regime but the asymptotic fo

declarations.name: CodingTheory.rs_epsCA_lower_capacity_bchks25_kk25
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 4.16 [BCHKS25, KK25].** Existence: for every `c > 0` and rate `ρ ∈ (0, 1/2)` there exists a power-of-two `n ∈ ℕ` and a Reed-Solomon code `C := RS[F, L, k]` of rate `ρ` over a prime field `F` with `|F| = poly(n)` and smooth `L` of size `n` such that `ε_ca(C, 1 - ρ - slack) ≥ n^c / |F|` for some `slack` of order `Θ(1/log n)`. We existentially bind the slack parameter as a real-valued knob rather than encoding `Θ` directly.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.rs_epsCA_breakdown_cs25
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 4.17 [CS25 Cor 1].** Complete CA breakdown for Reed-Solomon codes. Let `C := RS[F, L, k]` with `q = |F| ≥ 10`, rate `ρ`, and `δ` satisfying: `1 - H_q(δ) + 2/n + √((H_q(δ) - δ)/n) ≤ ρ ≤ 1 - δ - 2/n` Then `ε_ca(C, δ) = 1`. Uses `qEntropy` (ABF26 Definition 2.2, defined in `Basic/Entropy.lean`). Admitted as an external result.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.rs_epsCA_johnson_jump_bchks25
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 4.18 [BCHKS25 Cor 1.7].** CA jump at the Johnson bound. Fix `ε > 0`, let `δ := 15/16`. Then for all `F` of characteristic 2 there exists a Reed-Solomon code `C := RS[F, L, k]` with `n ≈ |F|^{(1+ε)/2}` and `δ_min(C) = 15/16` such that: `ε_ca(C, J(δ_min(C)), J(δ_min(C)) + 1/8 + 1/n) ≥ n^{2(1-ε)} / |F|` where `J(δ) := 1 - √(1 - δ)` is the Johnson radius. Witnesses a sharp jump in CA error precisely at the Johnson bound. **Note on `n ≈ |F|^{(1+ε)/2}`.** Paper writes equality but `|F|^{(1+ε)/2}` is generally not a natural number; the intended reading is "for `n` of this order of mag

declarations.name: CodingTheory.linear_epsCA_ge_sampling_dg25
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Lemma 4.19 [DG25 Thm 2.5].** Let `C ⊆ F^n` be a linear code and let `δ' := max_{u ∈ F^n} Δ(u, C)` be the (relative) covering radius. For every `δ ∈ (0, δ')`: `ε_ca(C, δ) ≥ ((q-1)/q) · Pr_{u ← F^n}[Δ(u, C) ≤ δ]` The probability is over a uniform word in `F^n`, expressed via the `Pr_{...}[...]` notation. Admitted as an external result.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.subspaceDesign_epsMCA_gg25
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 4.13 [GG25 Corollary 4.9].** τ-subspace-design codes have MCA bounds. Let `C : F^k → (F^s)^n` be a τ-subspace-design code. For every `t ∈ ℕ`: `ε_mca(C, 1 - τ(t+1) - 3/(2t)) ≤ (t·n + 4·t²) / |F|` Combined with `IsSubspaceDesign` (D2.16) and `subspaceDesign_tau_lower` (L2.17), this gives MCA up to capacity for subspace-design codes. Admitted as an external result.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.frs_epsMCA_capacity_gg25
declarations.doc: **ABF26 Theorem 4.14 [GG25 Corollary 4.10].** Folded Reed-Solomon codes have MCA up to capacity. Let `η ∈ (0, 1)` and `C := FRS[F, L, k, s, ω]` be a folded RS code with `s > 16/η²`. Then: `ε_mca(C, 1 - ρ - η) ≤ 2n/(η·|F|) + 24/(η³·|F|)` A corollary of T4.13 via T2.18 (FRS is τ-subspace-design). Admitted as an external result.

declarations.name: CodingTheory.subspaceDesign_epsMCA_polynomial_generators_bcgm25
declarations.doc: **ABF26 BCGM25 extension to T4.13 / T4.14 (polynomial generators preserve MCA).** [BCGM25] shows that MCA is preserved not only under affine line combinations `f₀ + γ · f₁` but under arbitrary *polynomial generators* — combinations of the form `f₀ + G(γ) · f₁` for a large class of functions `G : F → F` called "polynomial generators". Stated in ABF26 §4.2.2 (subsection on "subspace-design codes") as a parenthetical remark and footnote 2 of the introduction; not separately numbered as `T4.x`, but materially extends the reach of T4.13 / T4.14. The statement formalisation here follows the curve-MC

declarations.name: disagreementSet
declarations.signature: def disagreementSet (U₀ U₁ V₀ V₁ : InterleavedWord A (κ := κ) (ι := ι)) : Finset ι :=
declarations.doc: The set D = Δ^{2m}(U, V), columns where U₀≠V₀ or U₁≠V₁. Specialisation of the canonical `Code.disagreementCols` (in [Basic/Distance.lean](../../Basic/Distance.lean)) to the two-stack DG25 setting: a column belongs to D if *either* the first stack or the second stack disagrees there. Equivalently, `disagreementSet U₀ U₁ V₀ V₁ = Code.disagreementCols U₀ V₀ ∪ Code.disagreementCols U₁ V₁`.

declarations.name: affineWord_close_to_affineInterleavedCodeword

Statement: For any parameter `r` in `R*`, the matrix `Uᵣ` must be `e`-close to the codeword `Vᵣ`. Lemma 3.2: Give

declarations.name: card_agreeing_cells_in_D_le

Therefore, the total number of agreeing cells `(r, j)` with `j ∈ D` is at most `|D|`.

declarations.name: R_star_star_upper_bound
declarations.doc: **Lemma 3.3 (DG25): Upper Bound on R** Cardinality** Context: - `U₀, U₁` are columnWise words; `V₀, V₁` are columnWise codewords - `R_star` is the set where affine combinations `Uᵣ` are close to `C^m`. - `D` is the set of columns where `U₀` differs from `V₀` OR `U₁` differs from `V₁`. - `R_star_star` is the set of pairs `(r, j)` where `r ∈ R_star` and column `j` of `Uᵣ` equals column `j` of `Vᵣ` (set of `agreeing linearComb columns`) Statement: The number of agreeing column pairs `(r, j)` is bounded by the number of parameters in `R_star` times the number of columns *outside* `D`, plus the num

declarations.name: _anon_class_702
declarations.signature: class classical -- Use classical logic for decidable predicates on filters

declarations.name: R_star_star_lower_bound
declarations.doc: **Lemma 3.4 (DG25): Lower Bound on R** Cardinality** Context: - Same as Lemma 3.2, including hypotheses on `C`, `e`, `V₀`, `V₁`, `R_star`. - `R_star_star` is the set of pairs `(r, j)` where `r ∈ R_star` and column `j` of `Uᵣ` equals column `j` of `Vᵣ`. Statement: The number of agreeing column pairs `(r, j)` is at least the number of parameters in `R_star` times the number of columns guaranteed to agree (`n - e`) for each `r` (using Lemma 3.2), i.e. `|R**| ≥ (n - e) * |R*|`

declarations.name: e_mul_R_div_R_sub_1_lt_e_add_1_real
declarations.signature: lemma e_mul_R_div_R_sub_1_lt_e_add_1_real {e R : ℕ} (hR_gt_e_add_1 : e + 1 < R) :
declarations.doc: This lemma proves the final algebraic step in the DG25 Theorem 3.1 proof. It shows that if `R > e + 1`, then `e * (R / (R - 1)) < e + 1`. The intuition is that the fraction `R / (R - 1)` is always greater than 1, but as `R` gets larger, it gets closer to 1. The hypothesis `R > e + 1` provides a strong enough bound to ensure the product `e * (fraction)` doesn't "overshoot" `e + 1`.

We note that `R*` is precisely the set of parameters `r_{ϑ-1} ∈ 𝔽_q` for which the half-length matrix `(1-r_{ϑ-1}) · U₀ + r_{ϑ-1} · U₁` fulfills the inductive hypothesis (that is, the hypothesis of Definition 2.3, with respect to the smaller list size parameter)
