declarations.signature: def Straightline.append (E₁ : Extractor.Straightline oSpec Stmt₁ Wit₁ Wit₂ pSpec₁)
declarations.doc: The sequential composition of two straightline extractors. TODO: state a monotone condition on the extractor, namely that if extraction succeeds on a given query log, then it also succeeds on any extension of that query log

declarations.name: Extractor.RoundByRound.append
declarations.doc: The round-by-round extractor for the sequential composition of two (oracle) reductions

declarations.name: Verifier.StateFunction.append
declarations.doc: The sequential composition of two state functions.

declarations.name: Prover.append_run
declarations.doc: States that running an appended prover `P₁.append P₂` with an initial statement `stmt₁` and witness `wit₁` behaves as expected: it first runs `P₁` to obtain an intermediate statement `stmt₂`, witness `wit₂`, and transcript `transcript₁`. Then, it runs `P₂` on `stmt₂` and `wit₂` to produce the final statement `stmt₃`, witness `wit₃`, and transcript `transcript₂`. The overall output is `stmt₃`, `wit₃`, and the combined transcript `transcript₁ ++ₜ transcript₂`.

declarations.name: Verifier.append_run
declarations.doc: Running the sequential composition of two verifiers on a transcript of the combined protocol is equivalent to running the first verifier on the first part of the transcript, and the second verifier on the second part of the transcript, and returning the final statement.

declarations.name: Reduction.append_completeness
declarations.doc: Sequential composition preserves completeness Namely, two reductions satisfy completeness with compatible relations (`rel₁`, `rel₂` for `R₁` and `rel₂`, `rel₃` for `R₂`), and respective completeness errors `completenessError₁` and `completenessError₂`, then their sequential composition `R₁.append R₂` also satisfies completeness with respect to `rel₁` and `rel₃`. The completeness error of the appended reduction is the sum of the individual errors (`completenessError₁ + completenessError₂`).

declarations.name: Reduction.append_perfectCompleteness
declarations.signature: theorem append_perfectCompleteness (R₁ : Reduction oSpec Stmt₁ Wit₁ Stmt₂ Wit₂ pSpec₁)
declarations.doc: If two reductions satisfy perfect completeness with compatible relations, then their concatenation also satisfies perfect completeness.

declarations.name: Verifier.append_soundness
declarations.signature: theorem append_soundness {lang₁ : Set Stmt₁} {lang₂ : Set Stmt₂} {lang₃ : Set Stmt₃}
declarations.doc: If two verifiers satisfy soundness with compatible languages and respective soundness errors, then their sequential composition also satisfies soundness. The soundness error of the appended verifier is the sum of the individual errors.

declarations.name: Verifier.append_knowledgeSoundness
declarations.doc: If two verifiers satisfy knowledge soundness with compatible relations and respective knowledge errors, then their sequential composition also satisfies knowledge soundness. The knowledge error of the appended verifier is the sum of the individual errors.

declarations.name: Verifier.append_rbrSoundness
declarations.signature: theorem append_rbrSoundness {lang₁ : Set Stmt₁} {lang₂ : Set Stmt₂} {lang₃ : Set Stmt₃}
declarations.doc: If two verifiers satisfy round-by-round soundness with compatible languages and respective RBR soundness errors, then their sequential composition also satisfies round-by-round soundness. The RBR soundness error of the appended verifier extends the individual errors appropriately.

declarations.name: Verifier.append_rbrKnowledgeSoundness
declarations.doc: If two verifiers satisfy round-by-round knowledge soundness with compatible relations and respective RBR knowledge errors, then their sequential composition also satisfies round-by-round knowledge soundness. The RBR knowledge error of the appended verifier extends the individual errors appropriately.

declarations.name: OracleReduction.append_completeness
declarations.doc: Sequential composition preserves completeness Namely, two oracle reductions satisfy completeness with compatible relations (`rel₁`, `rel₂` for `R₁` and `rel₂`, `rel₃` for `R₂`), and respective completeness errors `completenessError₁` and `completenessError₂`, then their sequential composition `R₁.append R₂` also satisfies completeness with respect to `rel₁` and `rel₃`. The completeness error of the appended reduction is the sum of the individual errors (`completenessError₁ + completenessError₂`).

declarations.name: OracleReduction.append_perfectCompleteness
declarations.doc: If two oracle reductions satisfy perfect completeness with compatible relations, then their sequential composition also satisfies perfect completeness.

declarations.name: OracleVerifier.append_soundness
declarations.doc: If two oracle verifiers satisfy soundness with compatible languages and respective soundness errors, then their sequential composition also satisfies soundness. The soundness error of the appended verifier is the sum of the individual errors.

declarations.name: OracleVerifier.append_knowledgeSoundness
declarations.doc: If two oracle verifiers satisfy knowledge soundness with compatible relations and respective knowledge errors, then their sequential composition also satisfies knowledge soundness. The knowledge error of the appended verifier is the sum of the individual errors.

declarations.name: OracleVerifier.append_rbrSoundness
declarations.signature: theorem append_rbrSoundness (V₁ : OracleVerifier oSpec Stmt₁ OStmt₁ Stmt₂ OStmt₂ pSpec₁)
declarations.doc: If two oracle verifiers satisfy round-by-round soundness with compatible languages and respective RBR soundness errors, then their sequential composition also satisfies round-by-round soundness. The RBR soundness error of the appended verifier extends the individual errors appropriately.

declarations.name: OracleVerifier.append_rbrKnowledgeSoundness
declarations.signature: theorem append_rbrKnowledgeSoundness (V₁ : OracleVerifier oSpec Stmt₁ OStmt₁ Stmt₂ OStmt₂ pSpec₁)
declarations.doc: If two oracle verifiers satisfy round-by-round knowledge soundness with compatible relations and respective RBR knowledge errors, then their sequential composition also satisfies round-by-round knowledge soundness. The RBR knowledge error of the appended verifier extends the individual errors appropriately.

declarations.name: Prover.seqCompose
declarations.doc: Sequential composition of provers, defined via iteration of the composition (append) of two provers. Specifically, we have the following definitional equalities: - `seqCompose (m := 0) P = Prover.id` - `seqCompose (m := m + 1) P = append (P 0) (seqCompose (m := m) P)` TODO: improve efficiency, this might be `O(m^2)`

declarations.name: Verifier.seqCompose
declarations.doc: Sequential composition of verifiers, defined via iteration of the composition (append) of two verifiers. Specifically, we have the following definitional equalities: - `seqCompose (m := 0) V = Verifier.id` - `seqCompose (m := m + 1) V = append (V 0) (seqCompose (m := m) V)` TODO: improve efficiency, this might be `O(m^2)`

declarations.name: Reduction.seqCompose
declarations.signature: def seqCompose {m : ℕ} (Stmt : Fin (m + 1) → Type) (Wit : Fin (m + 1) → Type)
declarations.doc: Sequential composition of reductions, defined via sequential composition of provers and verifiers (or equivalently, folding over the append of reductions). TODO: improve efficiency, this might be `O(m^2)`

declarations.name: Reduction.seqCompose_zero
declarations.signature: lemma seqCompose_zero (Stmt : Fin 1 → Type) (Wit : Fin 1 → Type)

declarations.name: OracleProver.seqCompose
declarations.doc: Sequential composition of provers in oracle reductions, defined via sequential composition of provers in non-oracle reductions.

declarations.name: OracleVerifier.seqCompose'
declarations.doc: Sequential composition of verifiers in oracle reductions. This is the auxiliary version that has instance parameters as implicit parameters, so that matching on `m` can properly specialize those parameters. TODO: have to fix instance diamonds to make this work

declarations.name: OracleVerifier.seqCompose
declarations.doc: Sequential composition of oracle verifiers (in oracle reductions), defined via iteration of the composition (append) of two oracle verifiers.

declarations.name: OracleReduction.seqCompose
declarations.doc: Sequential composition of oracle reductions, defined via sequential composition of oracle provers and oracle verifiers.

declarations.name: Verifier.seqCompose_soundness
declarations.doc: If all verifiers in a sequence satisfy soundness with respective soundness errors, then their sequential composition also satisfies soundness. The soundness error of the seqComposed verifier is the sum of the individual errors.

declarations.name: Verifier.seqCompose_knowledgeSoundness
declarations.doc: If all verifiers in a sequence satisfy knowledge soundness with respective knowledge errors, then their sequential composition also satisfies knowledge soundness. The knowledge error of the seqComposed verifier is the sum of the individual errors.

declarations.name: Verifier.seqCompose_rbrSoundness
declarations.doc: If all verifiers in a sequence satisfy round-by-round soundness with respective RBR soundness errors, then their sequential composition also satisfies round-by-round soundness.

declarations.name: Verifier.seqCompose_rbrKnowledgeSoundness
declarations.doc: If all verifiers in a sequence satisfy round-by-round knowledge soundness with respective RBR knowledge errors, then their sequential composition also satisfies round-by-round knowledge soundness.

declarations.name: OracleVerifier.seqCompose_soundness
declarations.doc: If all verifiers in a sequence satisfy soundness with respective soundness errors, then their sequential composition also satisfies soundness. The soundness error of the sequentially composed oracle verifier is the sum of the individual errors.

declarations.name: OracleVerifier.seqCompose_knowledgeSoundness
declarations.doc: If all verifiers in a sequence satisfy knowledge soundness with respective knowledge errors, then their sequential composition also satisfies knowledge soundness. The knowledge error of the sequentially composed oracle verifier is the sum of the individual errors.

declarations.name: OracleVerifier.seqCompose_rbrSoundness
declarations.doc: If all verifiers in a sequence satisfy round-by-round soundness with respective RBR soundness errors, then their sequential composition also satisfies round-by-round soundness.

declarations.name: OracleVerifier.seqCompose_rbrKnowledgeSoundness
declarations.doc: If all verifiers in a sequence satisfy round-by-round knowledge soundness with respective RBR knowledge errors, then their sequential composition also satisfies round-by-round knowledge soundness.

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.Equiv

declarations.doc: Two protocol specifications are equivalent if they have the same number of rounds, same direction for each round, and an equivalence of types for each round.

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.Equiv.refl
declarations.signature: def refl (pSpec : ProtocolSpec n) : Equiv pSpec pSpec where

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.Equiv.symm
declarations.signature: def symm (eqv : Equiv pSpec pSpec') : Equiv pSpec' pSpec where

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.Equiv.equivTrans

declarations.name: Prover.processRound
declarations.doc: Prover's function for processing the next round, given the current result of the previous round, and a function for getting the challenge.

declarations.name: Prover.runToRound
declarations.doc: Run the prover in an interactive reduction up to round index `i`, via first inputting the statement and witness, and then processing each round up to round `i`. Returns the transcript up to round `i`, and the prover's state after round `i`.

declarations.name: Prover.runWithLogToRound
declarations.doc: Run the prover in an interactive reduction up to round `i`, logging all the queries made by the prover. Returns the transcript up to that round, the prover's state after that round, and the log of the prover's oracle queries. This basically just wraps `runToRound` with a logging oracle.

declarations.name: Prover.fst_map_simulateQ_loggingOracle_run
declarations.signature: lemma fst_map_simulateQ_loggingOracle_run {ι : Type} {spec : OracleSpec ι} {α : Type}

declarations.name: Prover.run
declarations.doc: Run the prover in an interactive reduction. Returns the output statement and witness, and the transcript. See `runWithLog` for a version that additionally returns the log of the prover's oracle queries.

declarations.name: Prover.runWithLog
declarations.doc: Run the prover in an interactive reduction, logging all the queries made by the prover. Returns the transcript, the output statement and witness, and the log of the prover's oracle queries. Note: this is just a wrapper around `run` that logs the queries made by the prover.

declarations.name: Verifier.run
declarations.doc: Run the (non-oracle) verifier in an interactive reduction. It takes in the input statement and the transcript, and return the output statement.

declarations.name: OracleVerifier.run
declarations.doc: Run the oracle verifier in the interactive protocol. Returns the verifier's output, including both the output statement and oracle statements, and the log of queries made by the verifier.

declarations.name: OracleVerifier.run_eq_run_verifier
declarations.doc: Running an oracle verifier then is equal to running its non-oracle counterpart

declarations.name: Reduction.run
declarations.doc: An execution of an interactive reduction on a given initial statement and witness. Consists of first running the prover, and then the verifier. Returns the full transcript, the output statement and witness from the prover, and the output statement from the verifier. See `Reduction.runWithLog` for a version that additionally returns the logs of the prover's and the verifier's oracle queries.

declarations.name: Reduction.runWithLog
declarations.doc: An execution of an interactive reduction on a given initial statement and witness. Consists of first running the prover, and then the verifier. Returns the full transcript, the output statement and witness from the prover, and the output statement from the verifier, along with the logs of the prover's and the verifier's oracle queries.

declarations.name: Monad.map_of_prod_fst_eq_prod_fst
declarations.signature: lemma Monad.map_of_prod_fst_eq_prod_fst {m : Type u → Type v} [Monad m] [LawfulMonad m]
declarations.doc: TODO: figure out a better name for this

declarations.name: Reduction.runWithLog_discard_logs_eq_run
declarations.doc: Logging the queries made by both parties do not change the output of the reduction

declarations.name: OracleReduction.run
declarations.doc: Run an interactive oracle reduction. Returns the full transcript, the output statement and witness, the log of all prover's oracle queries, and the log of all verifier's oracle queries to the prover's messages and to the shared oracle.

declarations.name: OracleReduction.runWithLog
declarations.doc: Run an interactive oracle reduction. Returns the full transcript, the output statement and witness, the log of all prover's oracle queries, and the log of all verifier's oracle queries to the prover's messages and to the shared oracle.

declarations.name: OracleReduction.run_eq_run_reduction
declarations.doc: Running an oracle reduction is equal to running its non-oracle counterpart

declarations.name: OracleReduction.runWithLog_eq_runWithLog_reduction
declarations.doc: Running an oracle reduction with logging of queries to the shared oracle is equal to running its non-oracle counterpart with logging of queries to the shared oracle

declarations.name: Reduction.id_run
declarations.doc: Running the identity or trivial reduction results in the same input statement and witness, and empty transcript.

declarations.name: Reduction.id_runWithLog
declarations.doc: Running the identity or trivial reduction, with logging of queries to the shared oracle, results in the same input statement and witness, empty transcript, and empty query logs.

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.HasMessageSize
declarations.signature: class HasMessageSize {n : ℕ} (pSpec : ProtocolSpec n) where
declarations.doc: Type class for protocol specifications to specify the size of each message as a natural number (to be interpreted as a vector of units `U` of the given size for some sponge unit `U`)

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.HasChallengeSize
declarations.signature: class HasChallengeSize {n : ℕ} (pSpec : ProtocolSpec n) where
declarations.doc: Type class for protocol specifications to specify the size of each challenge as a natural number (to be interpreted as a vector of units `U` of the given size for some sponge unit `U`)

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.numPermQueriesMessage
declarations.doc: Number of queries to the permutation oracle needed to absorb the `i`-th message of the protocol specification. This is `Lₚ(i)` in the paper (Equation 7).

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.totalNumPermQueriesMessage
declarations.doc: Total number of queries to the permutation oracle needed to absorb all messages of the protocol specification. This is `Lₚ` in the paper (Equation 8).

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.numPermQueriesChallenge
declarations.doc: Number of queries to the permutation oracle needed to absorb the `i`-th challenge of the protocol specification. This is `Lᵥ(i)` in the paper (Equation 7).

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.totalNumPermQueriesChallenge
declarations.doc: Total number of queries to the permutation oracle needed to absorb all challenges of the protocol specification. This is `Lᵥ` in the paper (Equation 8).

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.totalNumPermQueries
declarations.doc: Total number of queries to the permutation oracle needed to absorb all messages and challenges of the protocol specification. This is `L` in the paper (Equation 8).

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.duplexSpongeHybridOracle
declarations.doc: The oracle specification for duplex sponge Fiat-Shamir (Equation 16, written as `𝒟_Σ`). It is indexed over the challenge rounds of the protocol specification, and for each such round `i`: - The input is the input statement `stmtIn` and, for each `j < i` that is a message round, a vector of units of size `Lₚ(j)` (the number of queries to the permutation oracle needed to absorb the `j`-th message) - The output is a vector of units of size `Lᵥ(i)` (the number of queries to the permutation oracle needed to absorb the `i`-th challenge)

declarations.name: OracleSpec.duplexSpongeChallengeOracle
declarations.doc: The oracle specification for duplex sponge Fiat-Shamir (Definition 4.2, written as `𝒟_𝔖`). The index consists of `(h, p, p⁻¹)`, where: - `h : ByteArray → Vector U SpongeSize.C` is the hash function (assumed to be random oracle) (Note: input could be different from `ByteArray`) - `p : Vector U SpongeSize.N → Vector U SpongeSize.N` is the forward direction of the random permutation - `p⁻¹ : Vector U SpongeSize.N → Vector U SpongeSize.N` is the backward direction of the random permutation

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.Messages.deriveTranscriptDSFSAux
declarations.signature: def deriveTranscriptDSFSAux {ι : Type} {oSpec : OracleSpec ι} {StmtIn : Type}
declarations.doc: Auxiliary function for deriving the transcript up to round `k` from the (full) messages, via querying the permutation oracle for the challenges. This is used to define `deriveTranscriptDSFS`.

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.Messages.deriveTranscriptDSFS
declarations.signature: def deriveTranscriptDSFS {ι : Type} {oSpec : OracleSpec ι} {StmtIn : Type}
declarations.doc: Derive the full transcript from the (full) messages, via doing absorb / squeeze operations on the duplex sponge. Returns the final state of the duplex sponge and the full transcript

declarations.name: DuplexSpongeFS.BacktrackSequence
declarations.doc: A backtracking sequence (Definition 5.3) for a given hash-duplex-sponge oracle trace `tr` and final duplex-sponge state `s` consists of the following data: - An input statement `𝕩` - A list `inputState = [sᵢₙ, ...]` of input states - A list `outputState = [sₒᵤₜ, ...]` of output states subject to the following conditions: - The last of the input states is the given final state - There is one more input state than output state - The statement is queried with the hash, and returns the capacity of the first input state `(hash, 𝕩, inputState[0].capacitySegment) ∈ tr`

declarations.name: DuplexSpongeFS.BacktrackSequence.Index
declarations.doc: The associated indices (first occurrences in the trace) for a backtracking sequence

declarations.name: DuplexSpongeFS.BacktrackSequenceFamily
declarations.doc: A family of backtrack sequences, defined as a finite set of backtrack sequences such that no two sequences are strict subsets of each other

declarations.name: DuplexSpongeFS.backTrack
declarations.doc: The backtracking procedure in Section 5.2, which takes in: - the query-answer trace for the oracle `(h, p, p⁻¹)` - a state (vector of `N` units) And returns one of the following: - `none` - `err` - A result consisting of: an input statement, a round index `i ≤ n`, and the protocol messages up to round `i` NOTE: we do _not_ define the extra data structure `tr▵` as in the paper, as that is entirely derived from the actual trace and is only present for efficiency (which we do not plan to reason about) TODO: figure out the best way to encode the two errors (currently we encode `err` as the failure

declarations.signature: def basicFiatShamirGame (V : Verifier oSpec StmtIn StmtOut pSpec)
declarations.doc: First game for the key lemma: the basic Fiat-Shamir transform. We run the malicious prover, then the verifier, then returns: - the input statement (that the malicious prover chooses) - the output statement (that the verifier returns) - the messages / proof sent by the prover - the query log of the prover - the query log of the verifier

We run the malicious prover, then the verifier, then returns: - the input statement (that the malicious prover chooses) - the output statement (that the verifier returns) - the messages / proof sent by the prover - the query log of the prover - the query log of the verifier

declarations.name: DuplexSpongeFS.duplexSpongeToFSGameStatDist
declarations.doc: Lemma 5.1 in the paper: given the two games and the auxiliary procedures to transform the malicious prover and the query-answer traces, the two games have outputs that are statistically indistinguishable, up to an error term TODO: fully fill in this lemma

declarations.name: DuplexSpongeFS.LookaheadSequence
declarations.doc: A look-ahead sequence (Equation 14) of a given trace of forward permutation queries, and an initial state, consists of: - A list of input states - A list of output states subject to the following conditions: - The two list of states have the same length - The first input state is the given initial state ... TODO: refactor this to cut down on data (can just omit output states?)

declarations.name: DuplexSpongeFS.LookaheadSequenceFamily
declarations.doc: A family of look-ahead sequences (Equation 14), parametrized by a trace of forward permutation queries, an initial state, and a challenge round index `i`, is defined as a finite set of look-ahead sequences such that: - no two sequences are strict subsets of each other - the length of any sequence is at most the challenge size of the given challenge round `i`

declarations.name: DuplexSpongeFS.computeLookaheadSequenceFamily
declarations.doc: Procedure to compute the lookahead sequence family (Equation 14) TODO: nail down exactly what this is; can it fail?

declarations.name: DuplexSpongeFS.lookAhead
declarations.doc: The lookahead procedure in Section 5.2, which takes in: - A query-answer trace for the oracle `p` - A permutation state (vector of `N` units) - A round index `i` for a challenge round Then performs a probabilistic computation (allowing to sample units uniformly at random) returning one of the following: - `none` - `err` - An encoded verifier's challenge (vector of `chalSize i` units) TODO: figure out the best way to encode the two errors (currently we encode `err` as the failure of OracleComp, and `none` as `Option.none` inside)

declarations.name: DuplexSpongeFS.basicToDuplexSpongeFSTrace

declarations.name: HashStateWithInstructions
declarations.doc: A stateful hash object that interfaces with duplex sponges. Rust interface: ```rust #[derive(Clone)] pub struct HashStateWithInstructions<H, U = u8> where U: Unit, H: DuplexSpongeInterface<U>, { /// The internal duplex sponge used for absorbing and squeezing data. ds: H, /// A stack of expected sponge operations. stack: VecDeque<Op>, /// Marker to associate the unit type `U` without storing a value. _unit: PhantomData<U>, } ``` This structure maintains the sponge state and tracks expected operations to ensure protocol compliance.

declarations.name: HashStateWithInstructions.generateTag
declarations.doc: Generate a 32-byte tag from the domain separator bytes using Keccak. Rust implementation: ```rust fn generate_tag(iop_bytes: &[u8]) -> [u8; 32] { let mut keccak = Keccak::default(); keccak.absorb_unchecked(iop_bytes); let mut tag = [0u8; 32]; keccak.squeeze_unchecked(&mut tag); tag } ```

declarations.name: Statement.Lens
declarations.signature: def Statement.Lens (OuterStmtIn OuterStmtOut InnerStmtIn InnerStmtOut : Type)
declarations.doc: A lens for transporting input and output statements for the verifier of a (non-oracle) reduction. Consists of two functions: - `proj : OuterStmtIn → InnerStmtIn` : Transport input statements from the outer context to the inner context - `lift : OuterStmtIn → InnerStmtOut → OuterStmtOut` : Transport output statements from the inner context to the outer context, additionally relying on the outer input statement. This is exactly the same as a `PFunctor.Lens` between two monomials defined by the input and output statements (from the outer to the inner context).

declarations.doc: Transport input statements from the outer context to the inner context

declarations.name: Statement.Lens.lift
declarations.doc: Transport output statements from the inner context to the outer context, additionally relying on the input statements of the outer context.

It consists of two functions: - `projWit : OuterStmtIn × OuterWitIn → InnerWitIn`, which derives the inner input witness from the outer one, requiring also the outer input statement. - `liftWit : OuterStmtIn × OuterWitIn → InnerStmtOut × InnerWitOut → OuterWitOut`, which derives the outer output witness from outer input witness & the inner output one, requiring also the associated statements. The inclusion of the statements are necessary when we consider the full view of the prover. In practice as

declarations.name: Verifier.compatStatement
declarations.doc: Compatibility relation between the outer input statement and the inner output statement, relative to a verifier. We require that the inner output statement is a possible output of the verifier on the outer input statement, for any given transcript. Note that we have to existentially quantify over transcripts since we only reference the verifier, and there's no way to get the transcript without a prover.

declarations.name: Reduction.compatContext
declarations.doc: Compatibility relation between the outer input context and the inner output context, relative to a reduction. We require that the inner output context (statement + witness) is a possible output of the reduction on the outer input context (statement + witness).

declarations.name: Extractor.Straightline.compatWit
declarations.doc: Compatibility relation between the outer input witness and the inner output witness, relative to a straightline extractor. We require that the inner output witness is a possible output of the straightline extractor on the outer input witness, for a given input statement, transcript, and prover and verifier's query logs.

declarations.name: OracleInterface
declarations.doc: `OracleInterface` is a type class that provides an oracle interface for a type `Message`. It consists of: - a query type `Query`, - a response type `Response`, - a function `answer` that given a message `m : Message` and a query `q : Query`, returns a response `r : Response`. TODO: turn `(Query, Response)` into a general `PFunctor` (i.e. `Response : Query → Type`) This allows for better compositionality of `OracleInterface`, including (indexed) sum, instead of requiring indexed family of `OracleInterface`s. However, this won't be possible until `OracleSpec` is changed to be an alias for `PFunc

declarations.name: OracleInterface._anon_instance_143
declarations.signature: instance instance (priority := low) instTensorProd {α β : Type*}
declarations.doc: The tensor product oracle interface for the product of two types `α` and `β`, each with its own oracle interface, is defined as: - The query & response types are the product of the two query & response types. - The oracle will run both oracles and return the pair of responses. This is a low priority instance since we do not expect to have this behavior often. See `instProd` for the sum behavior on the interface.

declarations.name: OracleInterface.instProd
declarations.signature: instance instProd {α β : Type} [Oα : OracleInterface α] [Oβ : OracleInterface β] :
declarations.doc: The product oracle interface for the product of two types `α` and `β`, each with its own oracle interface, is defined as: - The query & response types are the sum type of the two query & response types. - The oracle will answer depending on the input query. This is the behavior more often assumed, i.e. when we send multiple oracle messages in a round. See `instTensor` for the tensor product behavior on the interface.

declarations.name: OracleInterface._anon_instance_176
declarations.signature: instance instance (priority := low) instTensorForall {ι : Type u} (v : ι → Type _)
declarations.doc: The indexed tensor product oracle interface for the dependent product of a type family `v`, indexed by `ι`, each having an oracle interface, is defined as: - The query & response types are the dependent product of the query & response types of the type family. - The oracle, on a given query specifying the index `i` of the type family, will run the oracle of `v i` and return the response. This is a low priority instance since we do not expect to have this behavior often. See `instProdForall` for the product behavior on the interface (with dependent sums for the query and response types).

declarations.name: OracleInterface.instProdForall
declarations.signature: instance instProdForall {ι : Type u} (v : ι → Type _) [O : ∀ i, OracleInterface (v i)] :
declarations.doc: The indexed product oracle interface for the dependent product of a type family `v`, indexed by `ι`, each having an oracle interface, is defined as: - The query & response types are the dependent product of the query & response types of the type family. - The oracle, on a given query specifying the index `i` of the type family, will run the oracle of `v i` and return the response. This is the behavior usually assumed, i.e. when we send multiple oracle messages in a round. See `instTensorForall` for the tensor product behavior on the interface.

declarations.name: OracleInterface.simOracle0

declarations.name: OracleInterface.simOracle
declarations.signature: def simOracle {ι : Type u} (oSpec : OracleSpec ι)
declarations.doc: Combines multiple oracle specifications into a single oracle by routing queries to the appropriate underlying oracle. Takes: - A base oracle specification `oSpec` - An indexed type family `T` with `OracleInterface` instances - Values of that type family Returns a stateless oracle that routes queries to the appropriate underlying oracle.

declarations.name: OracleInterface.simOracle2
declarations.signature: def simOracle2 {ι : Type u} (oSpec : OracleSpec ι)
declarations.doc: Combines multiple oracle specifications into a single oracle by routing queries to the appropriate underlying oracle. Takes: - A base oracle specification `oSpec` - Two indexed type families `T₁` and `T₂` with `OracleInterface` instances - Values of those type families Returns a stateless oracle that routes queries to the appropriate underlying oracle.

declarations.name: OracleInterface.distanceLE
declarations.doc: A message type together with a `OracleInterface` instance is said to have **oracle distance** (at most) `d` if for any two distinct messages, there is at most `d` queries that distinguish them, i.e. `#{q | OracleInterface.answer a q = OracleInterface.answer b q} ≤ d`. This property corresponds to the distance of a code, when the oracle instance is to encode the message and the query is a position of the codeword. In particular, it applies to `(Mv)Polynomial`.

declarations.name: distanceLE_mvPolynomial_degreeLE
declarations.doc: Schwartz-Zippel-type distance bound for multivariable polynomials of degree ≤ d. The bound is `Fintype.card σ * d * Fintype.card R ^ (Fintype.card σ - 1)` (the earlier bound `Fintype.card σ * d` was false; see counterexample in comment history).

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.challengeOracleInterface
declarations.doc: Turn each verifier's challenge into an oracle, where querying a unit type gives back the challenge. This is the default instance for the challenge oracle interface. It may be overridden by `challengeOracleInterface{SR/FS}` for state-restoration and/or Fiat-Shamir.

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.getChallenge
declarations.doc: Query a verifier's challenge for a given challenge round `i`, given the default challenge oracle interface `challengeOracleInterface`. This is the default version for getting challenges, where we query the default `challengeOracleInterface`, which accepts trivial input. In contrast, `getChallenge{SR/FS}` requires an input statement and prior messages up to that round.

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.challengeQueryImpl
declarations.signature: def challengeQueryImpl {pSpec : ProtocolSpec n} [∀ i, SampleableType (pSpec.Challenge i)] :
declarations.doc: Define the query implementation for the verifier's challenge in terms of `ProbComp`. This is a randomness oracle: it simply calls the `selectElem` method inherited from the `SampleableType` instance on the challenge types.

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.challengeOracleInterfaceSR
declarations.doc: The oracle interface for state-restoration and (basic) Fiat-Shamir. This is the version where we hash the input statement and the entire transcript up to the point of deriving a new challenge. To be precise: - The domain of the oracle is `Statement × pSpec.MessagesUpTo i.1.castSucc` - The range of the oracle is `pSpec.Challenge i` - The oracle just returns the challenge

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.srChallengeOracle
declarations.signature: def srChallengeOracle (Statement : Type) {n : ℕ} (pSpec : ProtocolSpec n) :
declarations.doc: The oracle interface for Fiat-Shamir. This is the (inefficient) version where we hash the input statement and the entire transcript up to the point of deriving a new challenge. To be precise: - The domain of the oracle is `Statement × pSpec.MessagesUpTo i.1.castSucc` - The range of the oracle is `pSpec.Challenge i` Some variants of Fiat-Shamir takes in a salt each round. We assume that such salts are included in the input statement (i.e. we can always transform a given reduction into one where every round has a random salt).

declarations.name: ProtocolSpec.srChallengeQueryImpl
declarations.doc: Define the query implementation for the state-restoration / (slow) Fiat-Shamir oracle (returns a challenge given messages up to that point) in terms of `ProbComp`. This is a randomness oracle: it simply calls the `selectElem` method inherited from the `SampleableType` instance on the challenge types. We may then augment this with `withCaching` to obtain a function-like implementation (caches and replays previous queries). For implementation with caching, we add `withCaching`. For implementation where the whole function is sampled ahead of time, and we answer with that function, see `srChalleng

declarations.doc: Sequential composition of a family of `ProtocolSpec`s, indexed by `i : Fin m`. Defined for definitional equality, so that: - `seqCompose !v[] = !p[]` - `seqCompose !v[pSpec₁] = pSpec₁` - `seqCompose !v[pSpec₁, pSpec₂] = pSpec₁ ++ₚ pSpec₂` - `seqCompose !v[pSpec₁, pSpec₂, pSpec₃] = pSpec₁ ++ₚ (pSpec₂ ++ₚ pSpec₃)` - and so on. declarations.name: Reduction.completeness
declarations.doc: A reduction satisfies **completeness** with regards to: - an initialization function `init : ProbComp σ` for some ambient state `σ`, - a stateful query implementation `impl` (in terms of `StateT σ ProbComp`) for the shared oracles `oSpec`, - an input relation `relIn` and output relation `relOut` (represented as sets), and - an error `completenessError ≥ 0`, if for all valid statement-witness pair `(stmtIn, witIn) ∈ relIn`, the execution between the honest prover and the honest verifier will result in a tuple `((prvStmtOut, witOut), stmtOut)` such that - `(stmtOut, witOut) ∈ relOut`, (the outpu

declarations.doc: A reduction satisfies **perfect completeness** if it satisfies completeness with error `0`.

declarations.name: Reduction.completeness_error_mono
declarations.signature: theorem completeness_error_mono {ε₁ ε₂ : ℝ≥0} (hε : ε₁ ≤ ε₂) :
declarations.doc: If a reduction satisfies completeness with error `ε₁`, then it satisfies completeness with error `ε₂` for all `ε₂ ≥ ε₁`.

declarations.name: Reduction.completeness_relIn_mono
declarations.doc: If a reduction satisfies completeness with error `ε` for some relation `relIn`, then it satisfies completeness with error `ε` for any relation `relIn'` that is a subset of `relIn`.

declarations.name: Reduction.completeness_relOut_mono
declarations.doc: If a reduction satisfies completeness with error `ε` for some relation `relIn`, then it satisfies completeness with error `ε` for any relation `relOut'` that is a superset of `relOut`.

declarations.name: Reduction.perfectCompleteness_eq_prob_one
declarations.doc: Perfect completeness means that the probability of the reduction outputting a valid statement-witness pair is _exactly_ 1 (instead of at least `1 - 0`).

declarations.name: Extractor.Straightline
declarations.doc: A straightline, deterministic, non-oracle-querying extractor takes in the output witness, the initial statement, the IOR transcript, and the query logs from the prover and verifier, and returns a corresponding initial witness. Note that the extractor does not need to take in the output statement, since it can be derived via re-running the verifier on the initial statement, the transcript, and the verifier's query log. This form of extractor suffices for proving knowledge soundness of most hash-based IOPs.

declarations.name: Verifier.soundness
declarations.signature: def soundness (langIn : Set StmtIn) (langOut : Set StmtOut)
declarations.doc: A reduction satisfies **soundness** with error `soundnessError ≥ 0` and with respect to input language `langIn : Set StmtIn` and output language `langOut : Set StmtOut` if: - for all (malicious) provers with arbitrary types for `WitIn`, `WitOut`, - for all arbitrary `witIn`, - for all input statement `stmtIn ∉ langIn`, the execution between the prover and the honest verifier will result in an output statement `stmtOut` that is in `langOut` is at most `soundnessError`. (technical note: since execution may fail, this is _not_ equivalent to saying that `stmtOut ∉ langOut` with probability at leas

declarations.name: Verifier.IsSound
declarations.signature: class IsSound (langIn : Set StmtIn) (langOut : Set StmtOut)
declarations.doc: Type class for soundness for a verifier

declarations.name: Verifier.knowledgeSoundness
declarations.signature: def knowledgeSoundness (relIn : Set (StmtIn × WitIn)) (relOut : Set (StmtOut × WitOut))
declarations.doc: A reduction satisfies **(straightline) knowledge soundness** with error `knowledgeError ≥ 0` and with respect to input relation `relIn` and output relation `relOut` if: - there exists a straightline extractor `E`, such that - for all input statement `stmtIn`, witness `witIn`, and (malicious) prover `prover`, - if the execution with the honest verifier results in a pair `(stmtOut, witOut)`, - and the extractor produces some `witIn'`, then the probability that `(stmtIn, witIn')` is not valid and yet `(stmtOut, witOut)` is valid is at most `knowledgeError`.

declarations.name: Verifier.IsKnowledgeSound
declarations.signature: class IsKnowledgeSound (relIn : Set (StmtIn × WitIn)) (relOut : Set (StmtOut × WitOut))
declarations.doc: Type class for knowledge soundness for a verifier

declarations.name: Verifier.Extractor.Straightline.IsMonotone
declarations.doc: An extractor is **monotone** if its success probability on a given query log is the same as the success probability on any extension of that query log.

declarations.name: Reduction.Simulator

declarations.doc: A simulator for a reduction needs to produce the same transcript as the prover (but potentially all at once, instead of sequentially). We also grant the simulator the power to program the shared oracles `oSpec`

declarations.name: Verifier.knowledgeSoundness_implies_soundness
declarations.doc: Knowledge soundness with knowledge error `knowledgeError < 1` implies soundness with the same soundness error `knowledgeError`, and for the corresponding input and output languages.

declarations.name: Verifier.rbrSoundness_implies_soundness
declarations.signature: theorem rbrSoundness_implies_soundness (langIn : Set StmtIn) (langOut : Set StmtOut)
declarations.doc: Round-by-round soundness with error `rbrSoundnessError` implies soundness with error `∑ i, rbrSoundnessError i`, where the sum is over all rounds `i`.

declarations.name: Verifier.rbrKnowledgeSoundness_implies_rbrSoundness
declarations.doc: Round-by-round knowledge soundness with error `rbrKnowledgeError` implies round-by-round soundness with the same error `rbrKnowledgeError`.

declarations.name: Verifier.rbrKnowledgeSoundness_implies_knowledgeSoundness
declarations.doc: Round-by-round knowledge soundness with error `rbrKnowledgeError` implies knowledge soundness with error `∑ i, rbrKnowledgeError i`, where the sum is over all rounds `i`.

declarations.name: Verifier.srSoundness_addSalt_implies_srSoundness_original
declarations.doc: State-restoration soundness for a protocol with added salts implies state-restoration soundness for the original protocol (with improved parameters?)

declarations.name: Verifier.srKnowledgeSoundness_addSalt_implies_srKnowledgeSoundness_original
declarations.doc: State-restoration knowledge soundness for a protocol with added salts implies state-restoration knowledge soundness for the original protocol with improved parameters.

declarations.name: Verifier.srSoundness_implies_soundness
declarations.doc: State-restoration soundness implies basic (straightline) soundness. This theorem shows that state-restoration security is a strengthening of basic soundness. The error is preserved in the implication.

declarations.name: Verifier.srKnowledgeSoundness_implies_knowledgeSoundness
declarations.doc: State-restoration knowledge soundness implies basic (straightline) knowledge soundness. This theorem shows that state-restoration knowledge soundness is a strengthening of basic knowledge soundness. The error is preserved in the implication.

declarations.name: Extractor.RoundByRoundOneShot
declarations.doc: A **one-shot** round-by-round extractor is a function that: - Takes in index `m : Fin (n + 1)` - Takes in the input statement `stmtIn : StmtIn` - Takes in a partial transcript up to round `m` - Takes in the prover's query log (TODO: refine this, verifier's query log as well?) and returns an input witness `witIn : WitIn`. This is the old definition of round-by-round extractor, which is less general than the new definition (i.e. the input witness is extracted immediately, "in one shot", unlike the general definition where the input witness is derived via intermediate witnesses).

declarations.name: Extractor.RoundByRoundOneShot.IsMonotone
declarations.signature: class RoundByRoundOneShot.IsMonotone (E : RoundByRoundOneShot oSpec StmtIn WitIn pSpec)
declarations.doc: A one-shot round-by-round extractor is **monotone** if its success probability on a given query log is the same as the success probability on any extension of that query log. TODO: refine this

declarations.name: Extractor.RoundByRound
declarations.doc: A **round-by-round extractor** is a tuple of algorithms that iteratively extracts the input witness from the output witness, through a series of intermediate witnesses (indexed by `m : Fin (n + 1)`). Formally, it contains the following components: - A proof `eqIn : WitMid 0 = WitIn` that the first intermediate witness type is equal to the input witness type - A function `extractMid : (m : Fin n) → StmtIn → Transcript m.succ pSpec` `→ WitMid m.succ → WitMid m.castSucc` that extracts the intermediate witness for round `m` from the intermediate witness for round `m+1`, using the transcript up to

declarations.name: Extractor.RoundByRoundOneShot.toRoundByRound
declarations.signature: def toRoundByRound (E : RoundByRoundOneShot oSpec StmtIn WitIn pSpec) :
declarations.doc: A one-shot round-by-round extractor can be converted to the general round-by-round extractor format, where all intermediate witness types are equal to the input witness type. Note that the converse is _not_ true: it's not possible in general to convert a general round-by-round extractor to a one-shot one.

declarations.name: ArityTree.Skeleton
declarations.doc: A tree skeleton of depth `n` with arities `ar : Fin n → ℕ`. At depth `0`, only a leaf is allowed. At depth `n+1`, a node has `ar 0` children, each a skeleton of depth `n` with arities reindexed by `Fin.succ`.

declarations.name: ArityTree.Data
declarations.doc: A tree with the given skeleton, storing values of type `α` at every node (root and internal nodes as well as leaves). This is the arity-generalized analogue of `BinaryTree.FullData` from `ToMathlib/Data/IndexedBinaryTree`.

declarations.name: starSkel
declarations.doc: Depth-1 "star" skeleton with `k` leaves under the root.

declarations.doc: Depth-2 data example with values at root, level-1, and leaves.

declarations.name: TranscriptTree.Data
declarations.doc: The dependent transcript data over a skeleton for `pSpec`. At the root of a nonempty protocol `(n+1)`, we store either a message or a challenge of round `0` (depending on `pSpec.dir 0`). The children are transcript data for the tail protocol `pSpec.drop 1` and the tail arities `fun i => ar i.succ`. At depth `0` (empty protocol), there is no data (we use `PUnit`).

declarations.name: Prover.StateRestoration.Soundness
declarations.doc: The type for the **state-restoration** prover in the soundness game. Such a prover has query access to challenge oracles that can return the `i`-th challenge, for all `i : pSpec.ChallengeIdx`, given the input statement and the transcript up to that point. It returns an input statement, and a full transcript of interaction. This is different from the state-restoration prover type in the knowledge soundness game, which additionally needs to output an output witness.

declarations.name: Prover.StateRestoration.KnowledgeSoundness
declarations.signature: def StateRestoration.KnowledgeSoundness (oSpec : OracleSpec ι) (StmtIn WitOut : Type)
declarations.doc: The type for the **state-restoration** prover in the knowledge soundness game. Such a prover has query access to challenge oracles that can return the `i`-th challenge, for all `i : pSpec.ChallengeIdx`, given the input statement and the transcript up to that point. It returns an input statement, a full transcript of interaction, and an output witness. Note that the output witness is an addition compared to the state-restoration soundness prover type.

declarations.name: OracleProver.StateRestoration.Soundness
declarations.doc: The type for the **state-restoration** oracle prover (in an oracle reduction) in the soundness game. This is a wrapper around the state-restoration prover type in the soundness game for the associated reduction.

declarations.name: OracleProver.StateRestoration.KnowledgeSoundness
declarations.doc: The type for the **state-restoration** oracle prover (in an oracle reduction) in the knowledge soundness game. This is a wrapper around the state-restoration prover type in the knowledge soundness game for the associated reduction.

declarations.name: srKnowledgeSoundnessGame

declarations.name: VectorIOR
declarations.doc: Vector Interactive Oracle Reductions. This is just a specialization of `OracleReduction` to the case where all messages and oracle statements are vectors over some alphabet, and there is no shared oracle. We do _not_ require the (oracle) statements and witnesses to be vectors as well, though this can be done if needed.

declarations.name: VectorIOP
declarations.doc: Vector Interactive Oracle Proofs This is just a specialization of `OracleProof` to the case where all messages and oracle statements are vectors over some alphabet. We do _not_ require the (oracle) statements and witnesses to be vectors as well, though this can be done if needed.

declarations.name: VectorIOR.IsSecure
declarations.doc: A vector IOR is **secure** with respect to input relation `relIn`, output relation `relOut`, and round-by-round knowledge error `ε_rbr` if it satisfies (perfect) completeness and round-by-round knowledge soundness with respect to `relIn`, `relOut`, and `ε_rbr`.

declarations.name: VectorIOP.IsSecure
declarations.doc: A vector IOP is **secure** with respect to relation `relation` and round-by-round knowledge error `ε_rbr` if it satisfies (perfect) completeness and round-by-round knowledge soundness with respect to `relation` and `ε_rbr`.

declarations.name: VectorIOP.IsSecureWithGap
declarations.doc: A vector IOP **of proximity** is **secure** with respect to completeness relation `completeRelation`, soundness relation `soundRelation`, and round-by-round knowledge error `ε_rbr` if it satisfies: - (perfect) completeness with respect to `completeRelation`, - round-by-round knowledge soundness with respect to `soundRelation` and `ε_rbr`.

declarations.doc: Get the full fiber list `(x₀, ..., x_{2 ^ steps-1})` which represents the joined fiber `(q⁽ⁱ⁺steps⁻¹⁾ ∘ ⋯ ∘ q⁽ⁱ⁾)⁻¹({y}) ⊂ S⁽ⁱ⁾` over `y ∈ S^(i+steps)`, in which the LSB repsents the FIRST qMap `q⁽ⁱ⁾`, and the MSB represents the LAST `q⁽ⁱ⁺steps⁻¹⁾`

declarations.doc: **qMap_fiber coefficient extraction**. The coefficients of `x = qMap_total_fiber(y, k)` with respect to `basis_x` are exactly the function that puts binary coeffs corresponding to bits of `k` in the first `steps` positions, and shifts `y`'s coefficients. This is the multi-step counterpart of `qMap_fiber_repr_coeff`.

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.generates_quotient_point_if_is_fiber_of_y
declarations.doc: `x` is in the fiber of `y` under `qMap_total_fiber` iff `y` is the iterated quotient of `x`. That is, for binary field, the fiber of `y` is exactly the set of all `x` that map to `y` under the iterated quotient map.

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.foldMatrix
declarations.signature: def foldMatrix (i : Fin r) (steps : Fin (ℓ + 1)) (h_i_add_steps : i.val + steps < ℓ + 𝓡)
declarations.doc: `M_y` matrix which depends only on `y ∈ S^(i+ϑ)`

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.iterated_fold
declarations.signature: def iterated_fold (i : Fin r) (steps : Fin (ℓ + 1)) (h_i_add_steps : i.val + steps < ℓ + 𝓡)
declarations.doc: Iterated fold over `steps` steps starting at domain index `i`.

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.iterated_fold_transitivity
declarations.doc: Transitivity of iterated_fold : folding for `steps₁` and then for `steps₂` equals folding for `steps₁ + steps₂` with concatenated challenges.

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.challengeTensorProduct
declarations.signature: def challengeTensorProduct (steps : ℕ) (r_challenges : Fin steps → L) : Vector L (2 ^ steps) :=
declarations.doc: Tensor product of challenge vectors : for a local fold length `steps`, ⨂_{j=0}^{steps-1}(1-r_j, r_j).

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.fiberEvaluationMapping
declarations.signature: def fiberEvaluationMapping (i : Fin r) (steps : ℕ) (h_i_add_steps : i.val + steps < ℓ + 𝓡)
declarations.doc: Evaluation vector [f^(i)(x_0) ... f^(i)(x_{2 ^ steps-1})]^T

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.localized_fold_matrix_form
declarations.signature: def localized_fold_matrix_form (i : Fin ℓ) (steps : ℕ) (h_i_add_steps : i.val + steps ≤ ℓ)
declarations.doc: Matrix-vector multiplication form of iterated fold : For a local `steps > 0`, `∀ i ∈ {0, ..., l-steps}`, `y ∈ S^(i+steps)`, `fold(f^(i), r_0, ..., r_{steps-1})(y) = [⨂_{j=0}^{steps-1}(1-r_j, r_j)] • M_y` `• [f^(i)(x_0) ... f^(i)(x_{2 ^ steps-1})]^T`, where the right-hand vector's values `(x_0, ..., x_{2 ^ steps-1})` represent the fiber `(q^(i+steps-1) ∘ ... ∘ q^(i))⁻¹({y}) ⊂ S^(i)`.

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.localized_fold_eval
declarations.signature: def localized_fold_eval (i : Fin ℓ) (steps : ℕ) (h_i_add_steps : i + steps ≤ ℓ)
declarations.doc: Wrapper of `localized_fold_matrix_form` with `fiber_eval_mapping` being specified explicitly.

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.iterated_fold_eq_matrix_form
declarations.signature: theorem iterated_fold_eq_matrix_form (i : Fin ℓ) (steps : ℕ) (h_i_add_steps : i + steps ≤ ℓ)
declarations.doc: **Lemma 4.9.** The iterated fold equals the localized fold evaluation via matmul form

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.fold_advances_evaluation_poly
declarations.doc: Lemma 4.13 : if f⁽ⁱ⁾ is evaluation of P⁽ⁱ⁾(X) over S⁽ⁱ⁾, then fold(f⁽ⁱ⁾, r_chal) is evaluation of P⁽ⁱ⁺¹⁾(X) over S⁽ⁱ⁺¹⁾. At level `i = ℓ`, we have P⁽ˡ⁾ =

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.extractMiddleFinMask
declarations.signature: def extractMiddleFinMask (v : (sDomain 𝔽q β h_ℓ_add_R_rate) ⟨0, by exact pos_of_neZero r⟩)
declarations.doc: Given a point `v ∈ S^(0)`, extract the middle `steps` bits `{v_i, ..., v_{i+steps-1}}` as a `Fin (2 ^ steps)`.

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.eqTilde
declarations.signature: def eqTilde {L : Type} [CommRing L] {ℓ : ℕ} (r r' : Fin ℓ → L) : L :=
declarations.doc: The equality polynomial eq̃(r, r') that evaluates to 1 when r = r' and 0 otherwise. This is used in the final sumcheck identity : s_ℓ = c · eq̃(r, r')

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.BBF_Code
declarations.signature: def BBF_Code (i : Fin (ℓ + 1)) : Submodule L ((sDomain 𝔽q β h_ℓ_add_R_rate)
declarations.doc: The Reed-Solomon code C^(i) for round i in Binary Basefold. For each i ∈ {0, steps, ..., ℓ}, C(i) is the Reed-Solomon code RS_{L, S⁽ⁱ⁾}[2^{ℓ+R-i}, 2^{ℓ-i}].

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.BBF_CodeDistance
declarations.signature: def BBF_CodeDistance (ℓ 𝓡 : ℕ) (i : Fin (ℓ + 1)) : ℕ :=
declarations.doc: The (minimum) distance d_i of the code C^(i) : `dᵢ := 2^(ℓ + R - i) - 2^(ℓ - i) + 1`

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.disagreementSet
declarations.signature: def disagreementSet (i : Fin ℓ) (steps : ℕ) [NeZero steps] (h_i_add_steps : i.val + steps ≤ ℓ)
declarations.doc: Disagreement set Δ : The set of points where two functions disagree. For functions f^(i+ϑ) and g^(i+ϑ), this is {y ∈ S^(i+ϑ) | f^(i+ϑ)(y) ≠ g^(i+ϑ)(y)}. `Set`-valued analogue of the canonical `Code.disagreementCols` (in `ArkLib/Data/CodingTheory/Basic/Distance.lean`): used here because the underlying `sDomain` need not be `Fintype` / `DecidableEq` at every call site. When both instances are available, the two coincide modulo `Set.toFinset`.

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.fiberwiseDisagreementSet
declarations.signature: def fiberwiseDisagreementSet (i : Fin ℓ) (steps : ℕ) [NeZero steps]
declarations.doc: Fiber-wise disagreement set Δ^(i) : The set of points y ∈ S^(i+ϑ) for which functions f^(i) and g^(i) are not identical when restricted to the entire fiber of points in S⁽ⁱ⁾ that maps to y.

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.fiberwiseDistance
declarations.signature: def fiberwiseDistance (i : Fin ℓ) (steps : ℕ) [NeZero steps] (h_i_add_steps : i.val + steps ≤ ℓ)
declarations.doc: Fiber-wise distance d^(i) : The minimum size of the fiber-wise disagreement set between f^(i) and any codeword in C^(i).

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.fiberwiseClose
declarations.signature: def fiberwiseClose (i : Fin ℓ) (steps : ℕ) [NeZero steps] (h_i_add_steps : i.val + steps ≤ ℓ)
declarations.doc: Fiberwise closeness : f^(i) is fiberwise close to C^(i) if 2 * d^(i)(f^(i), C^(i)) < d_{i+steps}

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.hammingClose
declarations.signature: def hammingClose (i : Fin (ℓ + 1)) (f : OracleFunction 𝔽q β
declarations.doc: Hamming closeness : f is close to C in Hamming distance if 2 * d(f, C) < d_i

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.uniqueClosestCodeword
declarations.doc: Unique closest codeword : If a function f^(i) is within the unique decoding radius of the code C^(i), then this gives the unique closest codeword using Berlekamp-Welch decoder.

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.fiberwise_dist_lt_imp_dist_lt_unique_decoding_radius
declarations.doc: if `d⁽ⁱ⁾(f⁽ⁱ⁾, C⁽ⁱ⁾) < d_{ᵢ₊steps} / 2` (fiberwise distance), then `d(f⁽ⁱ⁾, C⁽ⁱ⁾) < dᵢ/2` (regular code distance)

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.isCompliant
declarations.doc: Compliance condition (Definition 4.17) : For an index `i` that is a multiple of `steps`, the oracle `f_i` is compliant if it's close to the code fiber-wise, the next oracle `f_i_plus_steps` is close to its code, and their unique closest codewords are consistent with folding.

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.farness_implies_non_compliance
declarations.signature: lemma farness_implies_non_compliance (i : Fin ℓ) (steps : ℕ) [NeZero steps]
declarations.doc: Farness implies non-compliance. If `f_i` is far from its code `C_i`, it cannot be compliant. This follows directly from the contrapositive of `fiberwise_dist_lt_imp_dist_lt`.

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.foldingBadEvent
declarations.signature: def foldingBadEvent (i : Fin ℓ) (steps : ℕ) [NeZero steps] (h_i_add_steps : i + steps ≤ ℓ)
declarations.doc: Bad event for folding (Definition 4.19) : This event captures two scenarios where the random folding challenges undermine the protocol's soundness checks. For `i ∈ {0, ..., ℓ - steps}`, - In case `d⁽ⁱ⁾(f⁽ⁱ⁾, C⁽ⁱ⁾) < dᵢ₊steps / 2` : `Δ⁽ⁱ⁾(f⁽ⁱ⁾, f̄⁽ⁱ⁾) ⊄ Δ(fold(f⁽ⁱ⁾, rᵢ', ..., rᵢ₊steps₋₁'), fold(f̄⁽ⁱ⁾, rᵢ', ..., rᵢ₊steps₋₁'))` - In case `d⁽ⁱ⁾(f⁽ⁱ⁾, C⁽ⁱ⁾) ≥ dᵢ₊steps / 2` : `d(fold(f⁽ⁱ⁾, rᵢ', ..., rᵢ₊steps₋₁'), C⁽ⁱ⁺steps⁾) < dᵢ₊steps / 2`

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.QueryPhase.extractNextSuffixFromChallenge

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.QueryPhase.proximityChecksSpec
declarations.doc: This proposition declaratively captures the iterative logic of the verifier. For each repetition and each folding step, it asserts that the folded value of the function from level `i` must equal the value of the function from the oracle of the next level `i+ϑ`.

declarations.name: Binius.BinaryBasefold.CoreInteraction.foldKnowledgeError
declarations.signature: def foldKnowledgeError (i : Fin ℓ) (_ : (pSpecFold (L := L)).ChallengeIdx) : ℝ≥0 :=
declarations.doc: Definition of the per-round RBR KS error for Binary FoldFold. This combines the Sumcheck error (1/|L|) and the LDT Bad Event probability. For round i : rbrKnowledgeError(i) = err_SC + err_BE where - err_SC = 1/|L| (Schwartz-Zippel for degree 1) - err_BE = (if ϑ ∣ (i + 1) then ϑ * |S^(i+1)| / |L| else 0) where k = i / ϑ and |S^(j)| is the size of the j-th domain

declarations.name: Binius.RingSwitching.TensorAlgebra
declarations.signature: abbrev TensorAlgebra (K L : Type*) [Field K] [Field L] [Algebra K L] := L ⊗[K] L
declarations.doc: Tensor Algebra A = L ⊗_K L. Based on the spec, it's viewed as (2^κ)x(2^κ) arrays of K-elements. The imported TensorAlgebra file provides the leftAlgebra instances.

declarations.name: Binius.RingSwitching._anon_def_66
declarations.signature: def def φ₀ (L K : Type*) [Field K] [Field L] [Algebra K L] : L →+* TensorAlgebra K L where
declarations.doc: Column embedding φ₀: L → A as a ring homomorphism. φ₀(α) = α ⊗ 1, operates on columns.

declarations.name: Binius.RingSwitching._anon_def_77
declarations.signature: def def φ₁ (L K : Type*) [Field K] [Field L] [Algebra K L] : L →+* TensorAlgebra K L where
declarations.doc: Row embedding φ₁: L → A as a ring homomorphism. φ₁(α) = 1 ⊗ α, operates on rows.

declarations.name: Binius.RingSwitching.decompose_tensor_algebra_rows
declarations.signature: def decompose_tensor_algebra_rows {σ : Type*} (β : Basis σ K L)
declarations.doc: Decompose `ŝ` into row components `(ŝ =: Σ_{u ∈ {0,1}^κ} β_u ⊗ ŝ_u)`. This views `L ⊗ L` as a module over `L` (left action) and finds the coordinates of `ŝ` with respect to the basis lifted from `β`.

declarations.name: Binius.RingSwitching.decompose_tensor_algebra_columns
declarations.signature: def decompose_tensor_algebra_columns {σ : Type*} (β : Basis σ K L) (s_hat : L ⊗[K] L) : σ → L :=
declarations.doc: Decompose `ŝ` into column components `(ŝ =: Σ_{v ∈ {0,1}^κ} ŝ_v ⊗ β_v)`. This views `L ⊗ L` as a module over `L` (right action) and finds the coordinates of `ŝ` with respect to the basis lifted from `β`.

declarations.name: Binius.RingSwitching.packMLE
declarations.signature: def packMLE (β : Basis (Fin κ → Fin 2) K L) (t : MultilinearPoly K ℓ) :
declarations.doc: **Definition 2.1 (MLE packing)**. Packs a small-field multilinear `t` into a large-field multilinear `t'` by reinterpreting chunks of `2^κ` coefficients as single `L`-elements. For each `w ∈ {0,1}^ℓ'`, the evaluation `t'(w)` is defined as: `t'(w) := ∑_{v ∈ {0,1}^κ} t(v₀, ..., v_{κ-1}, w₀, ..., w_{ℓ'-1}) ⋅ β_v`

declarations.name: Binius.RingSwitching.unpackMLE
declarations.signature: def unpackMLE (β : Basis (Fin κ → Fin 2) K L) (t' : MultilinearPoly L ℓ') :
declarations.doc: **Unpacking a Packed Multilinear Polynomial**. Reverses the packing defined in `packMLE`. It reconstructs the small-field multilinear `t` from the large-field multilinear `t'`. The evaluation of `t` at a point `(v, w)` is recovered by taking the evaluation of `t'` at `w`, which is an element of `L`, and finding its `v`-th coordinate with respect to the basis `β`.

declarations.name: Binius.RingSwitching.componentWise_φ₁_embed_MLE
declarations.doc: **Component-wise `φ₁` embedding**. Takes a polynomial `t'` with coefficients in `L` and embeds it into a polynomial with coefficients in the tensor algebra `A` by applying `φ₁` to each coefficient. This is achieved by using `MvPolynomial.map`.

declarations.name: Binius.RingSwitching.compute_final_eq_value
declarations.doc: Decompose the final eq tensor `e := Σ_{u ∈ {0,1}^κ} eq̃(u, r'') ⨂ e_u`, where e_u is the row components of e. Then compute `Σ_{u ∈ {0,1}^κ} eq̃(u_0, ..., u_{κ-1}, r''_0, ..., r''_{κ-1}) ⋅ e_u`.

declarations.doc: The prover in a no-interaction reduction can be specified by a tuple of functions: - `mapStmt : StmtIn → OracleComp oSpec StmtOut` maps the input statement to an output statement - `mapWit : StmtIn → WitIn → OracleComp oSpec WitOut` maps the input witness to an output witness, depending on the input statement

declarations.name: RandomQuery.relIn
declarations.signature: def relIn : Set ((StmtIn × ∀ i, OStmtIn OStatement i) × WitIn) :=
declarations.doc: The input relation is that the two oracles are equal.

declarations.name: RandomQuery.relOut
declarations.signature: def relOut : Set ((StmtOut OStatement × ∀ i, OStmtOut OStatement i) × WitOut) :=
declarations.doc: The output relation states that if the verifier's single query was `q`, then `a` and `b` agree on that `q`, i.e. `answer a q = answer b q`.

declarations.name: RandomQuery.oracleProver
declarations.doc: The prover is trivial: it has no messages to send.  It only receives the verifier's challenge `q`, and outputs the same `q`. We keep track of `(a, b)` in the prover's state, along with the single random query `q`.

declarations.name: RandomQuery.oracleVerifier
declarations.doc: The oracle verifier simply returns the challenge, and performs no checks.

declarations.name: RandomQuery.oracleReduction
declarations.doc: Combine the trivial prover and this verifier to form the `RandomQuery` oracle reduction: the input oracles are `(a, b)`, and the output oracles are the same `(a, b)` its output statement also contains the challenge `q`.

declarations.name: RandomQuery.oracleReduction_completeness
declarations.doc: The `RandomQuery` oracle reduction is perfectly complete.

declarations.doc: The lookup relation. Takes in a collection of values and a table, both containers for elements of type `α`, and asserts that all elements in `values` are also in `table`. Note: this generic membership typeclass allows for multiple underlying data types for `Value` and `Table`, such as `List`, `Set`, `Finset`, `Array`, `Vector`, etc.

declarations.name: MemoryChecking.ReadOnly.relation
declarations.signature: def relation : Set (M × List (MemoryRead ι α)) :=
declarations.doc: The read-only memory checking relation. It takes a memory `mem` and a list of read operations `ops`. The relation holds if for each operation `op` in `ops`, `op.value` is consistent with the value at `op.index` in `mem` (i.e., `Memory.read mem op.index = op.value`).

declarations.name: MemoryChecking.ReadWrite.processMemoryOps
declarations.signature: def processMemoryOps (ops : List (MemoryOp ι α)) (startMem : M) : Option M :=
declarations.doc: Processes a list of memory operations `ops` on a starting memory state `startMem`, producing a final state. This function folds over `ops`, updating the memory state at each step: - For a `read` operation, it checks if the value in the operation is consistent with the current memory state. If not, it fails (returns `none`). - For a `write` operation, it updates the memory with the value from the operation. Returns `some` of the final memory state on success, or `none` on a failed consistency check.

declarations.name: MemoryChecking.ReadWrite.relation
declarations.signature: def relation : Set (M × M × List (MemoryOp ι α)) :=
declarations.doc: The read-write memory checking relation. It takes an initial memory `startMem`, a final memory `finalMem`, and a list of operations `ops`. The relation holds if executing `ops` on `startMem` is consistent and results in `finalMem`.

declarations.doc: A Plonk constraint system is a vector of `numGates` gates, each parametrized by the underlying ring `𝓡` and `numWires`, the number of wires.

declarations.name: Plonk.ConstraintSystem.accepts
declarations.doc: A constraint system accepts an input vector `x` if all of its gates accept `x`.

declarations.name: Plonk.ConstraintSystem.partition
declarations.signature: def partition (cs : ConstraintSystem 𝓡 numWires numGates) :
declarations.doc: The partition induced by a constraint system as defined in the Plonk paper. For `i ∈ [numWires]`, let `T_i ⊆ [3*numGates]` be the set of indices `j` such that `V_j = i`, where `V` is the flattened vector of all wire indices `(a,b,c)` from all gates. This creates a partition of `[3 * numGates]` based on which gates use each wire index.

declarations.name: Plonk.ConstraintSystem.perm
declarations.signature: def perm (cs : ConstraintSystem 𝓡 numWires numGates) : Equiv.Perm (Fin (3 * numGates)) := sorry
declarations.doc: The permutation corresponding to the partition induced by a constraint system.

declarations.doc: A constraint system is prepared for `ℓ` public inputs, for some `ℓ ≤ numGates, numWires`, if for all `i ∈ [ℓ]`, the `i`-th gate constrains the `i`-th wire to be some public value.

declarations.name: Plonk.relation
declarations.signature: def relation (cs : ConstraintSystem 𝓡 numWires numGates) (ℓ : ℕ) (hℓ : ℓ ≤ numWires) :
declarations.doc: To define a relation based on the constraint system, we extend it with: - A natural number `ℓ ≤ m` representing the number of public inputs - A subset `ℐ ⊂ [m]` of "public inputs" (assumed to be `{1,...,ℓ}` without loss of generality)

declarations.doc: The vector `𝕫` is the concatenation of the public input and witness variables

declarations.name: R1CS.relation
declarations.doc: The R1CS relation: `(A *ᵥ 𝕫) * (B *ᵥ 𝕫) = (C *ᵥ 𝕫)`, where `*` is understood to mean component-wise (Hadamard) vector multiplication.

declarations.name: R1CS.pad
declarations.doc: Pad an R1CS instance (on the right) from `sz₁` to `sz₂` with zeros. Note that this results in truncation if the second size is smaller than the first one.

declarations.name: R1CS.pad_preserves_relation
declarations.doc: Padding preserves the R1CS relation when `sz₁.m ≤ sz₂.m` (no row truncation), `sz₁.n_w ≤ sz₂.n_w` (no witness truncation), and `sz₁.n_x = sz₂.n_x` (same number of public variables). The last condition is essential: padding `stmt` and `wit` independently can misalign the combined vector `𝕫` if `n_x` changes.

declarations.name: RoundConsistency.roundConsistencyCheck
declarations.doc: The generalized round consistency check: checks that the Lagrange-interpolating polynomial through `pts` evaluates to `β` at the challenge `γ`. Used in FRI to verify that the next-round value equals the fold evaluated at the challenge. Implemented via `CompPoly.CPolynomial`'s computable Lagrange interpolation, so the check itself is computable.

declarations.name: RoundConsistency.generalised_round_consistency_completeness
declarations.doc: Completeness of the round consistency check. Given a polynomial `f`, challenge `γ`, and `n`-th roots of unity `ω`, when `f` is honestly evaluated at the scaled points `{ω i * s₀}`, the round consistency check succeeds with the value `(foldNth n f γ).eval (s₀^n)`. This establishes that the Lagrange interpolant through the evaluation points matches the n-way folding operation at the challenge point.
